user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous call (up, 0.45) was correct in direction: fed_assets rose again to 8,860,485 from 8,867,834 one observation back — actually the latest print (8,860,485) is slightly below the prior observation (8,867,834), a small dip, but the broader 4-week and 13-week changes remain strongly positive (+103,025 and +304,304 respectively), and the level sits essentially at the 52-week high (only 7,349 below it). The policy language confirms continued net asset purchases, but at a tapering pace: Treasury purchases cut from $80B/month equivalent path down to $20B/month and MBS to $10B/month beginning [date] following a prior cut from $40B/$20B. This is still QE — assets are still being added, not drained — but the pace of expansion is decelerating sharply toward zero. Given tapering is explicit and nearly complete, the balance sheet is still growing but at a much slower rate, and the single most recent observation ticked down slightly, which could be noise or the first sign of the taper's effect. Net acquisition pace is small and shrinking, consistent with expansion continuing through month-end but at a diminishing rate, not yet flat or reversing.
Would change its mind if: A subsequent month-over-month decline in fed_assets, or language announcing purchases reduced to zero/runoff, would flip this to flat or down.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior "flat" call at 0.35 conviction was wrong: the spread has now broken decisively below 0.70, hitting a new 12-month low of 0.61, exactly the falsifier condition I set for renewed flattening. The last three readings (0.63, 0.63, 0.61) show flattening momentum has not stalled, with slope_change_21d at -0.18pp and slope_change_63d at -0.46pp both confirming a persistent, accelerating flattening trend rather than noise. The spread sits at slope_range_position 0.00 — the very bottom of its 12-month range — with slope_distance_from_flat down to just 0.61pp, meaning inversion risk is now much closer. Policy language has shifted hawkish: guidance moved from tapering-in-progress to "soon appropriate to raise the target range," which historically compresses front-end-to-belly spreads faster than the long end, consistent with the observed flattening. The 10s30s slope at +0.32pp remains modestly positive, suggesting the flattening pressure is concentrated in the 2s10s segment rather than a broad long-end selloff. With no signs of stabilization in the last three prints and hawkish rhetoric intensifying, I expect continued flattening into month-end, though the pace of decline may moderate somewhat given how compressed the spread already is.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above 0.70 sustained for 2+ readings, or slope_change_21d turning positive, would signal flattening has stalled.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_distance_from_flat, slope_10s30s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has essentially stabilized after its earlier tightening drift. Over the last 13 weeks the index rose modestly (+0.08) from very loose levels, but the most recent 4 weeks show a slight reversal (-0.01), with the last several prints clustered tightly between -0.54 and -0.56. The latest value (-0.55) sits right at the middle of this recent range, and the range_position of 0.90 shows we're near the high (tighter) end of the 52-week span, but momentum has flattened rather than continuing to build. My prior flat call was directionally correct — the anticipated resumption of a clear multi-week rise did not materialize, nor did a drop back toward -0.60. The series is oscillating in a narrow band with no clear trend. Policy language remains unchanged, describing conditions as "accommodative," consistent with the very negative distance-from-zero reading (-0.55), but this is stale boilerplate and not informative for the one-month change. Given the flattening momentum and lack of a decisive directional push in either the measurements or the policy text, I maintain a flat call, with slightly higher conviction than before since the range has now held for several consecutive weeks without breaking.
Would change its mind if: A break below -0.60 (renewed easing) or a sustained multi-week climb toward -0.50 or higher (renewed tightening) would change this call.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last 10 breakevens (2.46→2.44) show stabilization after an early dip, not a renewed uptrend. The series bottomed near 2.33 mid-window, rebounded to 2.38-2.44, and has now printed flat for two consecutive readings (2.44, 2.44). My prior "up" call was wrong in direction of magnitude — the 21d and 63d changes are both modestly negative (-0.14, -0.13 pp), showing the medium-term trend is still net down from where it was a month/quarter ago, even though the most recent few prints have leveled off. The 252d change (+0.31pp) shows expectations remain well above where they were a year ago, and the range position (0.51) sits right in the middle of the 12-month band, arguing against a strong directional signal either way. Distance from target remains firmly above 2% (+0.44pp), consistent with expectations anchored moderately above objective, but that's a level story, not a momentum one. Given the flattening in the last two prints after the dip, and negative but decelerating short/medium-term momentum, I don't see strong evidence for continued upward drift. The most defensible call now is essentially flat, with a slight lean toward stabilization rather than a resumed rise.
Would change its mind if: A clear break above 2.50 or below 2.35 sustained over multiple observations would confirm a genuine directional move rather than range-bound flatness.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.62
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was correct: headline CPI y/y rose further to 7.56% from 7.17%, and core PCE y/y climbed to 5.21% from 4.89%, extending an unbroken multi-month acceleration. The falsifier I set (two consecutive sub-0.3% mom prints plus core PCE turning down) did not occur — mom actually reaccelerated: 0.86 → 0.69 → 0.60, still well above the 0.72% 3-month average and far above any level consistent with 2% annualized inflation. The 3m annualized headline pace (8.95%) is running hotter than the 12m y/y rate (7.56%), and core 3m annualized (6.64%) likewise exceeds its own y/y print, meaning momentum is not decelerating even though the single latest mom reading (0.60) is modestly below the immediately preceding two. Policy language confirms this isn't yet seen as easing: officials shifted from calling imbalances "transitory" to acknowledging inflation "has exceeded 2 percent for some time," and began tapering asset purchases — a clear acknowledgment of sustained overheating. Core is running far above target (+3.21pp) and the range position is at the top of its 12-month band (1.00). While the deceleration in mom from 0.86 to 0.60 is a mild yellow flag worth watching, it's not enough on its own to break the trend given still-elevated 3m annualized rates and rising y/y in both series. I still expect headline CPI y/y to rise next month.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print below 0.3% combined with core PCE y/y turning down and 3m annualized headline falling below the 12m y/y rate.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom, headline_mom_3m_avg, yoy_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print rose to 4.00% from 3.90%, an uptick of 0.10pp after a long, steep decline (-2.40pp over 12 months). This confirms my prior falsifier condition — I said a print at or above 4.00-4.10% would signal stalling, and that's exactly what happened. The unemployment rate is now essentially at the 3m average (4.00%) and just 0.10pp above the 12-month low, so the Sahm-gap remains tiny (+0.10pp) and doesn't yet indicate a genuine trend reversal or rapid deterioration. However, the multi-month streak of consecutive declines has clearly broken — this is the first uptick after six straight prints of falling or flat readings. Policy language still describes "substantial" declines and "solid" job gains, but that language reflects the trend through the prior prints, not this newest uptick, and is now being used to justify tightening (tapering asset purchases), consistent with labor conditions being assessed as near maximum employment rather than still loosening rapidly. Given the level is at the natural rate (distance_from_natural ≈ 0) and the increase is small but real, I read this as a stalling of the tightening trend rather than a resumption of loosening — a flat-to-slightly-up call with modest conviction, not a confident reversal.
Would change its mind if: A print above 4.20% or a Sahm-gap exceeding 0.50pp would confirm a genuine loosening trend rather than a stall near the natural rate.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, sahm_gap, unrate_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_3m_avg

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call is validated: dgs10 has climbed from 1.75 to 1.79 recently after a dip, with 21d change +0.27pp and 63d +0.22pp, both positive. The 12-month range position sits at 0.90, meaning the yield is near the top of its trailing range, and it's 0.70pp above its 12m low versus only 0.08pp below the 12m high. The 252d change of +0.68pp confirms a strong sustained uptrend over the past year. The 10s30s slope of +0.32pp is not inverted, consistent with continued long-end pressure rather than curve flattening from flight-to-quality. Policy language confirms continued tapering of Treasury purchases (now down to $20bn/month from $40bn), which reduces central bank demand for long-end paper and should keep upward pressure on term premium via reduced balance-sheet support. This is a incremental continuation of the previously announced taper, not a reversal, so it reinforces rather than changes the up call. Momentum has been choppy short-term (1.85→1.81→1.78→1.79) but the broader trend and level near range highs support continued modest upward pressure into month-end.
Would change its mind if: A sustained multi-session drop below 1.70 with negative 21d momentum would signal easing term-premium pressure and reverse this call.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace and composition of future runoff/taper decisions directly drive long-end supply pressure.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment could trigger flight-to-quality that would offset taper-driven upward pressure.
raw_response: {"notes": "The wedge trade rests on inflation (realised) vs inflation_expectations, with labor_tightness as a cross-read on persistence. Inflation is running hot and accelerating (headline 7.56%, core PCE 5.21%, 3m annualized still above y/y), but that reading is 16 days old and, more importantly, breakevens themselves have already stabilized and rolled over from their highs: 21d and 63d changes are negative, the last two prints are flat at 2.44, and range position sits mid-band. This is exactly the \"hot realised inflation, anchored expectations\" signature the framework flags \u2014 the market is not extrapolating the hot CPI/PCE prints into higher long-run compensation. Labor tightness gives a corroborating signal: unemployment ticked up to 4.00% after a long decline, the first uptick in six months, suggesting the labor market is stalling near the natural rate rather than tightening further. That undercuts the wage-driven persistence story that would be needed to keep pushing breakevens wider \u2014 if labor slack stops shrinking, the case for embedding higher inflation into expectations weakens further. So even though realised inflation is objectively hotter, the two-pronged read (anchored breakevens + stalling labor tightness) argues the inflation wedge is more likely to flatten or drift narrower than widen further from here, not accelerate. I am not fully confident in a narrowing call given the CPI momentum is genuinely strong and could eventually feed through with a lag, but the expectations series and labor cross-read both point away from further widening. This is a moderate downgrade from any prior \"up\" breakeven stance toward flat-to-slightly-narrower, driven by the expectations analyst's own stabilization, not a single hot print.</notes>\n<parameter name=\"drivers\">[{\"driver\":\"inflation\",\"conviction\":0.62,\"why\":\"Headline CPI and core PCE both accelerating, 3m annualized hotter than y/y; realised inflation clearly rising.\"},{\"driver\":\"inflation_expectations\",\"conviction\":0.0,\"why\":\"10y breakeven flat for two prints, negative 21d/63d momentum, mid-range position; no directional signal, offsets hot CPI.\"},{\"driver\":\"labor_tightness\",\"conviction\":0.1,\"why\":\"First uptick in unemployment after long decline; slack stopped shrinking, mild lean toward loosening, undercuts persistence case.\"}]", "leaned_on": ["inflation_expectations", "labor_tightness"], "discounted": [{"driver": "inflation", "why": "Hot and accelerating, but breakevens already show this is not translating into wider expectations."}], "falsifier": "A clear break of 10y breakeven above 2.50 sustained over multiple prints, or unemployment resuming its decline below 3.90%, would flip this back toward widening.", "confidence": 0.45, "risks": [{"tag": "policy", "text": "If Fed under-delivers on hawkish signaling, hot realised inflation could eventually de-anchor expectations and widen breakevens."}, {"tag": "positioning", "text": "Breakeven stabilization could be a pause before resuming uptrend given still-strong CPI momentum."}, {"tag": "liquidity", "text": "Thin month-end liquidity in TIPS market could exaggerate small moves in either direction."}], "trade": {"legs": [{"instrument": "T10YIE", "weight": -0.2}], "conviction": 0.35, "rationale": "Expectations have stalled/rolled over despite hot CPI, and labor tightness cross-read weakens persistence case; lean toward mild narrowing, not aggressive short."}}

arbitrated.notes: The wedge trade rests on inflation (realised) vs inflation_expectations, with labor_tightness as a cross-read on persistence. Inflation is running hot and accelerating (headline 7.56%, core PCE 5.21%, 3m annualized still above y/y), but that reading is 16 days old and, more importantly, breakevens themselves have already stabilized and rolled over from their highs: 21d and 63d changes are negative, the last two prints are flat at 2.44, and range position sits mid-band. This is exactly the "hot realised inflation, anchored expectations" signature the framework flags — the market is not extrapolating the hot CPI/PCE prints into higher long-run compensation. Labor tightness gives a corroborating signal: unemployment ticked up to 4.00% after a long decline, the first uptick in six months, suggesting the labor market is stalling near the natural rate rather than tightening further. That undercuts the wage-driven persistence story that would be needed to keep pushing breakevens wider — if labor slack stops shrinking, the case for embedding higher inflation into expectations weakens further. So even though realised inflation is objectively hotter, the two-pronged read (anchored breakevens + stalling labor tightness) argues the inflation wedge is more likely to flatten or drift narrower than widen further from here, not accelerate. I am not fully confident in a narrowing call given the CPI momentum is genuinely strong and could eventually feed through with a lag, but the expectations series and labor cross-read both point away from further widening. This is a moderate downgrade from any prior "up" breakeven stance toward flat-to-slightly-narrower, driven by the expectations analyst's own stabilization, not a single hot print.

discounted.why: Hot and accelerating, but breakevens already show this is not translating into wider expectations.

arbitrated.falsifier: A clear break of 10y breakeven above 2.50 sustained over multiple prints, or unemployment resuming its decline below 3.90%, would flip this back toward widening.

risks.text: If Fed under-delivers on hawkish signaling, hot realised inflation could eventually de-anchor expectations and widen breakevens.
Breakeven stabilization could be a pause before resuming uptrend given still-strong CPI momentum.
Thin month-end liquidity in TIPS market could exaggerate small moves in either direction.

trade.rationale: Expectations have stalled/rolled over despite hot CPI, and labor tightness cross-read weakens persistence case; lean toward mild narrowing, not aggressive short.

user_prompt:
Your previous meeting
  You called: inflation +0.62, inflation_expectations +0.00, labor_tightness +0.10
  The position you are carrying: T10YIE -0.20 (conviction 0.35).
  You said this read would flip if: A clear break of 10y breakeven above 2.50 sustained over multiple prints, or unemployment resuming its decline below 3.90%, would flip this back toward widening.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print confirms continued balance-sheet expansion: total assets rose to 8,928,129 from 8,911,033, a fresh 52-week high, consistent with my prior "up" call, which was correct. The 4-week change (+67,644) and 13-week change (+246,358, +2.84%) both remain positive, though the pace is clearly decelerating — the taper is visibly working, with monthly purchase caps stepping down from $80bn+ combined to just $30bn combined (Treasuries $20bn + MBS $10bn) starting [date] down from $40bn/$20bn the prior month. This is still net QE, just at a shrinking rate. With purchases still positive but being halved again, assets should keep rising into month-end, but by a smaller increment than the trailing 4-week pace suggests, since the new lower monthly pace only partially reflects in the data shown. No language yet signals a move to zero purchases or runoff, so I expect continued (slowing) growth in assets, not a reversal. Reserve creation continues, just at a decelerating clip, which is the "why" behind the still-positive but flattening curve of increases.
Would change its mind if: A month-over-month decline in fed_assets, or language announcing an end to purchases/shift to runoff, would flip this to flat or down.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct: slope fell from 0.42 to 0.39, continuing the flattening trend. The 2s10s spread sits at 0.39pp, essentially at its 12-month low (0.38) with range_position of just 0.01 — meaning the curve is near the flattest point of the past year. All momentum measures confirm this: 21d change -0.24, 63d change -0.59, 252d change -0.91, all negative and accelerating over longer windows. The policy language has shifted decisively hawkish — from tapering to an explicit signal that a hike is imminent ("expects it will soon be appropriate to raise the target range"), with asset purchases ending in early [date] . This front-loads tightening expectations, pressuring the front end (2Y) higher relative to the long end, which is the classic mechanism for flattening. The 10s30s slope at 0.34 shows the long end itself is not steepening either, consistent with a broad-based flattening rather than a front-end-only phenomenon. With the spread already pinned near cycle lows and hawkish rhetoric intensifying rather than stabilizing, there is no evidence of a rebound — my stated falsifier (sustained readings above 0.70, or 21d turning positive) has not been met. I expect continued modest flattening or stabilization at a compressed level.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above 0.55 sustained for 2+ readings, or slope_change_21d turning positive, would signal flattening has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_10s30s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI has moved from -0.55 to -0.47 over the last several weeks, a clear and steady rise. The 4-week change (+0.09) and 13-week change (+0.10) are consistent with each other, indicating this is not noise but a sustained trend of tightening momentum. The index is now sitting at its 52-week high, with range_position at 1.00 - the top of its recent band - confirming this is the tightest reading in the observed window, even though the level (-0.47) remains below the zero average, so conditions are still net accommodative in absolute terms. My prior flat call was wrong: at that point the climb toward -0.50 was already underway and has now cleared through that threshold, which I had flagged as my trigger to reverse. The steady, monotonic [date] upward over the last several observations (particularly the last 4: -0.54, -0.52, -0.49, -0.47) shows accelerating tightening momentum rather than stabilization. Policy language remains static and accommodative-oriented, but the measurement itself is the graded signal and it points unambiguously higher. I now call this up with good conviction given the consistency across both short and medium windows.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back below -0.52 or flattening over 2+ weeks would suggest the tightening trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_high, nfci

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call has been invalidated: breakeven_10y broke decisively above 2.50 and has continued climbing to 2.62, the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.77, only 0.14pp below the 12m high of 2.76). The last two readings (2.54→2.62) show acceleration, not consolidation, and the 21-day change (+0.22pp) is now much larger than the 63-day change (+0.07pp), indicating the move is recent and gaining pace rather than fading. The 252-day change (+0.47pp) confirms a sustained uptrend over the year, not noise. Distance from the 2% objective is now +0.62pp, well above where it sat a few observations ago, suggesting market-implied inflation expectations are becoming increasingly unanchored to the upside relative to target. With price action pushing to fresh highs within the range and momentum building rather than decelerating, the evidence supports continuation over reversion. I was wrong to stay flat last time — the multi-observation break above 2.50 that I set as my falsifier has now occurred emphatically. I'll shift to a clear "up" call, tempered only by the fact that breakevens are near the top of their historical range and could see some mean-reversion resistance there.
Would change its mind if: A pullback below 2.50 sustained over 2-3 observations, or a flattening of the 21-day change toward zero, would signal the uptrend has stalled.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.68
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 13 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was correct: headline CPI rose from 7.56% to 7.94% y/y, and every underlying measure confirms continued acceleration rather than any turn. Core PCE climbed again to 5.36% from 5.21%, still rising monotonically for 13 straight readings with no sign of plateauing. The 3m annualized rates for both headline (8.25%) and core (6.65%) remain well above the trailing y/y rates, meaning recent momentum is running hotter than the reported year-over-year figure — a strong signal that the y/y print itself has further to climb before base effects catch up. The outgoing MoM print (0.52%) is below the 3-month average (0.66%), but at 0.52% it's still a very hot monthly pace in absolute terms, nowhere near the sub-0.3% threshold that would have flagged deceleration. Policy language shifted meaningfully: the Committee moved from "inflation having exceeded 2 percent for some time" tapering language to explicitly "inflation well above 2 percent" with tightening now imminent — confirming officials see continued overheating, not moderation. Core-headline gap remains wide but core is still accelerating, which is the more persistent signal. All evidence — momentum, level, and policy characterization — points the same direction with no offsetting signal.
Would change its mind if: A headline MoM print below 0.3% combined with core PCE y/y turning down and 3m annualized headline falling below the trailing y/y rate.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom, headline_mom_3m_avg

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 20 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print ticked down to 3.90% from 4.00%, matching the 12-month low and putting the unemployment rate at the bottom of its 13-month range (range position 0.00). The Sahm gap is essentially nil (+0.03pp), confirming no loosening signal whatsoever. The 3m change (-0.20pp) and 6m change (-1.20pp) both show continued tightening, not stabilization. My prior "flat" call was proven wrong by this data — the market kept tightening rather than stalling near the natural rate. The level is now 0.10pp below my assumed natural rate anchor of 4%, indicating a historically tight labor market with no sign of turning. Policy language also confirms this: statements shifted from acknowledging "further improvement in the labor market" to declaring a "strong labor market" as justification for raising rates soon — language consistent with a labor market at or beyond full employment, not one showing cracks. Momentum has been consistently downward (declining unemployment) for months. Absent a clear reversal signal, the most likely near-term outcome is continued flatness at this very low level, with a modest risk of a further tick down given the persistent trend, rather than any reversal upward.
Would change its mind if: A print above 4.10% or a Sahm-gap exceeding 0.30pp would signal a genuine turn toward loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print shows a sharp drop to 1.83 from 1.97, a notable one-observation decline after several months of gradual increases (63d +0.35pp, 252d +0.39pp). My prior "up" call was directionally right on the medium-term trend but the most recent data point is a pullback, not a continuation. Still, 21d change is only +0.02pp — essentially flat over the past month — so the single-day drop hasn't yet produced sustained negative momentum. The yield sits at 0.74 of its 12-month range (well above the low), still much closer to the top than the bottom, and the 10s30s slope remains a healthy +0.34pp, consistent with ongoing term-premium richness rather than collapse. Policy language continues to taper asset purchases (further $20bn/$10bn reduction beginning in the new month), which reduces official-sector demand for long-end paper and structurally supports higher term premium over time, even though the latest print dipped. Given the tapering continues and the level remains high in its range, I lean toward the drift resuming upward over the next month, but the recent dip and flat 21d change temper confidence. This is a lean, not a strong call.
Would change its mind if: A confirmed multi-session move below 1.75 with negative 21d momentum and flattening 30s10s slope would indicate the term-premium uptrend has genuinely reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Confirm pace and market absorption of continued taper/runoff, key driver of long-end supply pressure.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk-off or flight-to-quality flows could explain the single-day yield drop.
raw_response: {"notes": "The key change since last meeting is that inflation_expectations has flipped from flat to a clear, accelerating \"up\" call: breakevens broke decisively above 2.50 (the exact level I set as my own falsifier last time) and are now at 2.62, near the top of the 12-month range, with 21d momentum outpacing 63d momentum \u2014 i.e., the move is recent and building, not stale or mean-reverting yet. This is precisely the signal I said would flip my prior narrowing call. Realised inflation is also hot and accelerating (CPI 7.94%, core PCE still rising for 13 straight months), but per the arbitration rule, when realised inflation is hot and expectations are the one that moves, I resolve toward expectations rather than splitting the difference \u2014 and here expectations aren't just anchored-despite-hot-inflation, they're now confirming and amplifying it. That's the strongest possible case for widening breakevens, not the classic divergence case. arbitrated.notes:
The key relevant analysts for the breakeven wedge are inflation, inflation_expectations, and labor_tightness. Inflation (realised) remains hot and accelerating — headline CPI 8.57%, core PCE 5.60%, both at fresh highs with 3m annualized rates above y/y, confirming no peak yet. That's a strong, high-conviction realised print. But per the panel-design signature case, hot realised inflation with expectations rolling over should resolve toward expectations, not a split. And here inflation_expectations has genuinely flipped: the prior call (up, 0.65) was explicitly wrong-footed — three consecutive breakeven declines (2.95→2.86→2.86→2.84), a clear near-term rollover even though longer 63d/252d windows still show net rise. This is a fresh, live read directly on the instrument I trade (10y breakeven itself), so it should dominate the realised-inflation print, which mostly reflects backward-looking CPI/PCE and is already reflected in current breakeven pricing.

Labor tightness is a supporting cross-read: unemployment at a fresh 12m low (3.70%), Sahm gap low, no reversal signal — a tight labor market that argues for continued wage/inflation persistence pressure, which would normally support sticky or rising expectations. This creates some tension with the expectations reversal, but the direct market-based signal (actual breakeven prints turning down three times in a row) is a more trade-relevant, timely read than the labor proxy, so I weight the direct expectations data more heavily.

Net: the fresh expectations reversal is the dominant, actionable signal against a backdrop of already-priced hot realised inflation. This argues for reducing my prior widening view — not fully reversing to narrow, but stepping down conviction and trimming the long-breakeven position, since the expectations turn is the one driver that actually moves T10YIE and it has visibly cracked.

discounted.why: Hot realised CPI is backward-looking and largely already priced into breakevens; expectations reversal is the live signal.

arbitrated.falsifier: A bounce in 10y breakeven back above 2.90 sustained for 2+ observations, or renewed acceleration in 21d momentum, would revert this to a widening call.

risks.text: Three-observation reversal in breakevens is thin evidence to fully abandon the prior widening thesis.
Fed hawkish pivot (runoff, hiking) could suppress breakevens mechanically via real-yield channel, muddying the pure expectations read.
If realised inflation keeps surprising hot, expectations could snap back upward quickly, reversing this trim.

trade.rationale: Trim prior +0.50 widening position toward near-neutral given the fresh expectations rollover, while retaining a small long bias since realised inflation pressure and labor tightness still argue against outright narrowing.

why.inflation: CPI/PCE both accelerating to fresh highs, 3m annualized above y/y — persistence confirmed, no peak yet.

why.labor_tightness: Unemployment fell to fresh 12m low, Sahm gap benign — labor market tightening, own-measurement falling clearly.

Last meeting I ran a narrowing wedge (T10YIE +0.20 net long breakevens, but leaning heavily on a falling-expectations call at -0.25 combined with hot realized inflation +0.75). That expectations call has now been explicitly falsified: breakeven_10y bounced through 2.90 for 2+ observations (2.88, 2.97) exactly per my own stated trigger, and the inflation_expectations analyst has flipped from down(-0.45) to up(+0.35), citing be_change_63d/252d both near +0.45pp and range_position 0.82 (near 12m highs). That is precisely the flip condition I set at the prior meeting, so I must respect it rather than anchor to the old call.

Meanwhile realized inflation has softened at the margin — headline CPI y/y actually ticked down for the first time in the series, though core PCE is still creeping at cycle highs and MoM deceleration is only single-print evidence. This is the classic case where realized and expectations diverge, and per my mandate I resolve toward the expectations driver, not a split. The panel explicitly flags inflation lacks the labor cross-read; but labor_tightness is flat/plateaued (3.70%, no further tightening), which offers no wage-push acceleration story to reinforce inflation persistence — it's a neutral input, not a strong lean either way, so I don't override on it.

Given expectations turned constructive and are the primary driver of breakevens (a live, repriced signal from actual TIPS-implied inflation compensation, not a slow-moving structural variable), while inflation itself is only modestly softening and not confirmed by core, I move from a modest net-long breakeven position toward a firmer widening view. This is a genuine turn in the driver that matters most for this trade, not mere price momentum — it satisfies my own pre-specified falsifier.

arbitrated.notes: The three drivers I actually trade on for the breakeven wedge are inflation (realised), inflation_expectations, and labor_tightness (as a cross-read on persistence). Inflation_expectations this meeting is a self-correction of last month's mis-scaled call: breakeven_10y never dropped toward 2.85 as feared, but rather stabilized in a 2.55-2.60 band and has ticked up modestly for three straight prints (2.62→2.63→2.64). That's a real, if tentative, sign that the expectations anchor is drifting mildly higher, not falling — conviction only 0.30, reflecting genuine near-term data rather than a confident structural call, which is exactly the kind of live signal that should move breakevens. Realised inflation printed hot on headline (8.54% y/y, 0.95% MoM) but core PCE actually ticked down slightly and its 3m momentum is flat — a headline/core divergence that looks like base-effect/energy noise rather than a fresh acceleration in underlying persistence. Per the arbitration rule, hot realised inflation with a comparatively calmer expectations read should resolve toward expectations, not split the difference or chase the headline print. Labor tightness provides a useful cross-read: unemployment at a fresh 12-month low (3.60%) with consistently negative 3m/6m/12m changes suggests genuine underlying labor-market tightness that could sustain wage-driven inflation persistence, lending some support to the case that inflation compensation has room to widen rather than compress, reinforcing the expectations lean rather than contradicting it. Weighing all this: modest, fresh upward drift in expectations, corroborated by tight labor conditions, outweighs a headline-driven, core-decelerating inflation print. This supports maintaining a modest long-breakeven stance, similar to before but slightly lower conviction given the expectations analyst downgraded its own conviction from 0.35 to 0.30.

  My prior "up" call is confirmed by the newest print: headline CPI y/y rose again to 8.98% from 8.54%, and the MoM print jumped to 1.26%, well above the falsifier threshold of 0.4% and above the 3-month average of 0.84%. Headline 3m-annualized is a very hot 10.52%, and yoy_range_position sits at the top of its 12-month range, confirming headline momentum remains strong, not turning. Core PCE y/y, however, continues to soften — 5.18% vs 5.60% two months prior, a -0.42pp 3-month change, with core 3m-annualized at 4.47%, well below headline's pace. This divergence (core_minus_headline at -3.80pp) is mostly energy/food-driven per the still-unchanged policy language citing "higher energy prices" and the invasion's upward pressure on inflation. Since headline is what I'm asked to forecast next, and its momentum (accelerating MoM, rising yoy) dominates the surprise, I weight headline heavily for the next print despite core's gentle cooling. The policy language shift (dropping "likely to weigh" for "are weighing," adding "strongly committed") signals persistent concern, not resolution. Next headline CPI y/y is more likely to rise or stay elevated than to fall meaningfully.
Would change its mind if: A headline MoM print below ~0.4%, with core PCE continuing its decline, would confirm the turn and flip me to down/flat.

Call: down, conviction 0.30

Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate printed 3.60%, unchanged from the prior reading and at the 12-month low, confirming my prior "down" call and its stated falsifier condition (print at or below 3.60% = continued tightening). The market is at its tightest point in the observed window, sitting 0.40pp below the assumed natural rate of ~4%. Momentum measures show continued mild tightening: -0.10pp over 3m, -0.30pp over 6m, and a steep -2.30pp over 12m, though the pace of decline has clearly decelerated compared to the earlier part of the series (which fell from 5.90% to 3.90% rapidly, then flattened into a narrow 3.60-4.00% band over the last seven readings). The Sahm gap is near zero (+0.03pp), so there's no recession-signal loosening underway. Policy language reinforces this, noting "unemployment rate has declined substantially" and upgrading job gains from "strong" to "robust," suggesting continued labor market strength. With the rate now flat at its floor for two consecutive readings and range position at 0 (the low end), the most likely next print is either another 3.60% or a marginal uptick, but there's no evidence yet of a turning point. I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down, but the room for further declines is shrinking given proximity to structural floors.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70% would signal a turn toward loosening; continued readings at or below 3.60% confirm tightening persists.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

Given the tension between still-elevated momentum measures and the recent one-observation drop, I lean toward continued modest upward pressure but with reduced conviction given the fresh pullback.

This is a fresh, live reversal, not noise — exactly the falsifier condition I set myself.

Meanwhile realised inflation (headline CPI) stayed hot — 8.98% y/y, MoM 1.26%, well above the outgoing pace. The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.

The 4-week change is -23,549 and 13-week change is -49,195, both negative and roughly consistent in pace with continued QT runoff — the series shows a clear step-down over the last several observations rather than noise around a flat trend. The 52-week change remains positive (+668,531), reflecting earlier expansion/backstop operations, but that's a stale signal for a one-month-ahead call; recent pace dominates per mandate. Policy language is unchanged, reaffirming continued balance-sheet runoff via capped Treasury/agency redemptions, which supports a continued modest monthly decline rather than reversal. There's no evidence of any new liquidity facility or QE language. Given the consistent multi-week decline, below-average level, and unchanged runoff policy, I now expect assets to be lower at next month-end, continuing the recent trend rather than reversing.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in fed_assets back above the 13w average for multiple consecutive weeks, or statement language pausing/ending balance-sheet runoff.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_13w_avg

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call played out: slope_2s10s printed a fresh 12-month low at -0.22pp, exactly at the 12m low (slope_above_12m_low = 0.00, range_position = 0.00). The trend remains firmly negative across horizons — 21d change -0.26pp, 63d -0.44pp, 252d -1.27pp — all confirming persistent, accelerating flattening pressure with no sign of stabilization. The last print did bounce from -0.17 to -0.22, i.e., a fresh push flatter after two prints near -0.17/-0.18 that had looked like a pause. That pause failed; the curve made a new low instead of rebounding, which is the opposite of what would have triggered a reversal in my framework. Continued Fed tightening language (further target range hikes "anticipated") is consistent with front-end yields (dgs2 +2.89%) outrunning the back end (dgs10 +2.67%), keeping the curve pinned near cycle-flat levels. The 10s30s slope at +0.33pp shows the long end itself is not inverted, so this flattening is concentrated in the belly/front relationship, but that's outside my read. With the curve sitting at its 12-month low and downward momentum intact at every horizon, I expect renewed flattening or continued sub-flat readings into month end.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above -0.10pp with slope_change_21d turning positive would signal flattening pressure has reversed into steepening.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_12m_low, slope_range_position

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The series had tightened steadily from -0.34 to -0.16 over roughly 13 weeks, and I called that trend "up" (tightening) with 0.65 conviction. The three most recent prints (-0.17, -0.18, -0.20) show that trend has reversed: two consecutive weekly declines (increasingly negative, i.e. easing) after the -0.16 high, which is exactly the falsifier I specified. So the prior "up" call was wrong for this most recent leg — conditions eased over the last three readings rather than continuing to tighten. That said, the level remains near the top of its 52-week range (range_position 0.93) and well above the 52w low, so conditions are still historically loose in absolute terms but the momentum has clearly turned. The 4-week change is now -0.03 (easing), even though the 13-week change is still +0.15 (net tightening over the quarter). Given the multi-week reversal and the explicit falsifier being triggered, I now lean toward continued easing (nfci moving lower) over the next month, though conviction is moderate given only three data points confirm the reversal and the longer-term trend was tightening.
Would change its mind if: A new weekly print above -0.16 (new 52w high) or resumption of consecutive weekly increases would signal tightening has resumed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===

Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "down" call has been invalidated. Breakeven_10y has risen from 2.36 to 2.42 to 2.48 to 2.53 over the last four readings — a sustained, accelerating move well above the 2.45 threshold I set as my falsifier, and for more than two observations. The 21-day change is now +0.17pp, reversing the prior 63-day decline of -0.44pp. This is a clear trend reversal, not noise: three consecutive increases of similar magnitude (0.06, 0.06, 0.05) suggest momentum rather than a single data blip. Breakeven_range_position at 0.35 shows we're still below the 12-month high (3.02%) but well above the 12-month low (2.27%), and the distance from the 2% target has widened to +0.53pp, the highest in this window. Policy language remains unchanged (2% longer-run objective, broad information set including inflation expectations), so this move is coming from market pricing, not a shift in stated policy framework. Given the strength and persistence of the recent upmove, I now expect breakeven_10y to be higher at month-end, continuing the recent trajectory rather than reverting to the prior downtrend I had flagged.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 2.45 sustained for 2+ observations would signal the reversal has failed and the prior downtrend may resume.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_distance_from_target, be_range_position

Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)

My prior "up" call was wrong — headline yoy did still tick up to 8.46% from 8.23% two months back, but that reflects base effects on old high monthly prints rolling through, not current momentum, which has clearly broken. Core PCE yoy also ticked up to 5.32% from 5.18%, but the 3-month annualized core (5.28%) is below the recent yoy level and core_yoy_change_3m is -0.28pp, consistent with a rolling-over trend after peaking near 5.60%. headline_3m_annualized at 9.10% is noisy and inflated by earlier months' spikes, not by the latest -0.01% print, so I weight the single-month reading and the easing 3m-core trend more heavily than the stale yoy headline number. With energy/commodity effects likely fading and the very weak monthly print, I expect next month's headline yoy to decelerate from 8.46%, absent a large upside surprise. Policy language still stresses elevated inflation and firming policy, but no new upside surprise language appeared.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in headline MoM back above ~0.6% or core PCE yoy accelerating further above 5.35% would reverse this call.
Leaned on: headline_mom, outgoing_mom, headline_mom_3m_avg, core_yoy_change_3m, core_3m_annualized

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35

Report:
  The latest print of 3.50% came in below the prior 3.60% reading, confirming the "down" call from last time — the unemployment rate continued to tighten rather than turn. This is now at the 12-month low, with unrate_above_12m_low at 0.00pp and range_position at 0, meaning the series sits at the bottom of its trailing range. The pace of decline has decelerated sharply, however: 3m change is only -0.20pp and 6m change is -0.50pp, versus a much larger -1.90pp over 12m, indicating the rapid tightening phase is fading into a plateau near cycle lows. Sahm-gap is a negligible +0.07pp, showing no hint of the rapid-rise loosening signal. Distance from natural rate is -0.50pp, meaning the market is modestly tighter than the assumed 4% natural rate. Policy language shifted from "declined substantially" to "remained low," which describes deceleration/stabilization rather than reversal — consistent with the flattening trend in the data. Given the market has essentially stopped falling and is bouncing along 3.50-3.60%, I expect the unemployment rate to be roughly flat at the next report, with only a slight tilt toward continued marginal tightness given momentum has not reversed. Conviction is modest given the flattening trend removes the strong directional signal I had previously.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70% would confirm a turning point toward loosening; a print at or below 3.50% would suggest tightening is still active, not just flat.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_above_12m_low, unrate_range_position, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior "up" call at 0.35 conviction was wrong: the 10y has fallen from 3.04 to 2.67 over the recent window, with -0.43pp over 21d and -0.18pp over 63d, meeting the falsifier I set (sustained move below 2.90 with negative momentum). The 252d change of +1.42pp shows the broader multi-quarter uptrend remains intact, and range position (0.64) still sits above the midpoint of the 12-month band, so this isn't a collapse in term premium, just a pullback within an elevated range. The 30s10s slope at +0.33pp is positive and not flattening/inverting, so no clear reversal signal there either. Continued balance-sheet runoff language is unchanged, which is a structural upward pressure on long-end premium, but the recent price action clearly points down for now. Given the sharp and consistent recent decline (both 21d and 63d negative, with newest print the lowest in the series), the near-term momentum favors a continuation of lower yields into month-end rather than a snap-back, though the still-elevated range position and ongoing QT keep me from having high conviction on the downside.

arbitrated.notes: The prior meeting's short-breakeven call (T10YIE -0.50) was built on inflation_expectations falling and headline inflation cooling. This meeting's panel directly contradicts that: inflation_expectations analyst reports breakeven_10y has risen for four straight readings (2.36→2.42→2.48→2.53), breaking clean through the 2.45 threshold I explicitly set as my own falsifier, with accelerating 21d momentum (+0.17pp) reversing the prior -0.44pp 63d decline. That is exactly the flip condition I pre-committed to. Meanwhile realised inflation continues to decelerate (headline MoM -0.01%, 3m core annualized rolling over from its peak) — so we have the textbook divergence case: expectations moving one way, realised inflation the other. Per mandate, when realised and expectations diverge, resolve toward expectations, not a blend. Labor tightness is a supporting cross-read for inflation persistence: unemployment is at a 12-month low but decelerating in its descent (3m -0.20pp vs 12m -1.90pp), essentially plateauing near cycle lows. That's neutral-to-mild-tightening for wage/inflation persistence, not a strong offset to the breakeven signal, and it's a slower-moving, more anticipated driver anyway. The trade-relevant driver here is the live breakeven print itself — inflation_expectations — which is a direct read on the instrument I'm trading, not an inference. I am reversing my position: the falsifier I set was triggered squarely, this is a sustained multi-observation move, not a single print, and the divergence pattern matches the panel's own guidance for resolving toward expectations. I move to a moderate-conviction long breakeven position.

Reversing a carried short position costs on whipsaw if the rally stalls quickly.
Unchanged Fed language on 2% target could reassert anchor and cap further breakeven widening.
Thin month-end liquidity could exaggerate breakeven moves in either direction, distorting the signal.

trade.rationale: Expectations analyst's own pre-set falsifier triggered with sustained, accelerating breakeven rise; divergence from cooling realised inflation resolved toward expectations per mandate. Reverse short to moderate long.

why.labor_tightness: Unemployment at 12m low but descent decelerating/plateauing — mild persistent tightness, supports some inflation stickiness but not decisive.

arbitrated.notes: Last meeting I ran breakevens wide on a divergence case: realised inflation cooling but expectations (breakeven_10y itself) turning up, so I overrode toward expectations and went +T10YIE at 0.60 conviction. This meeting the expectations analyst has reversed itself entirely — the "up" call is now explicitly retracted, with breakeven_10y falling three straight readings to a fresh local low of 2.48, 63d change decisively negative (-0.17pp), and range position down to 0.27 (lower third of 12m range). That analyst itself says it was wrong last time and the breakdown below 2.50 is a genuine new declining trend, not noise. This is exactly my own stated falsifier condition (sustained drop, now multiple consecutive prints) triggering. Realised inflation also confirms down (headline decelerating sharply, core softening more modestly), so the divergence that justified my prior override has closed — both drivers now point the same way, down. Labor tightness is flat/mixed (one-month uptick in unemployment against a still-tight 6m trend) and gives no meaningful cross-read pulling against the expectations move; it's not informative enough to lean on here. Since expectations is the dominant driver for breakevens and it has flipped decisively from up to down, and realised inflation is no longer diverging but confirming, I flip my position from wide to narrow. This isn't a single hot/cool print — it's three consecutive lower expectations readings plus a regime-consistent inflation read, which is the kind of persistence-vs-noise resolution my override rule calls for.

So the disinflation case did not clearly restore, but headline still moved down more than I expected given a sticky core.
  
  Looking at momentum: headline_3m_annualized (4.26%) is now running below the 12m trailing y/y (7.76%), consistent with further y/y deceleration mechanically as high base months roll off. But the latest MoM print (0.56%) reaccelerated from the two prior very-low prints (0.06, 0.42), and core PCE y/y is rising again, its third straight increase from 5.11 to 5.36 to 5.61 — this is the most hawkish core signal in the whole series and argues persistence, not disintegration, of underlying price pressure. Headline's y/y decline is being driven by base effects and volatile components rather than genuine disinflation, since core is diverging upward. Given the tension — headline trending down, core accelerating — I lean toward the base-effect arithmetic continuing to pull headline y/y down modestly next print, but with reduced conviction given core's inflection upward signals underlying stickiness that could stall further headline declines.
Would change its mind if: A rise in headline MoM above 0.8% or core PCE y/y continuing above 5.6% would signal renewed broad-based acceleration, invalidating the down call.

Distance from a 4% natural rate is -0.40pp, meaning the market is still tighter than normal. Policy language softened slightly (dropped "nonetheless") but still affirms robust job gains and a low unemployment rate, consistent with continued tightness rather than any turning point. I see no rapid-rise signal and no level breakout. I'll maintain a flat call, tilting my next trigger levels tighter given the two-sided bounce pattern already observed at 3.60%.

My prior call (breakevens narrowing, -0.50 T10YIE) was built on inflation_expectations at -0.55 conviction, which this meeting's evidence flatly contradicts: the falsifier I set (sustained break above 2.30) was breached and breakeven_10y is now running 2.51-2.52, up from 2.44-2.45, with 21d momentum +0.33pp. That analyst explicitly flags its own prior call as wrong and flips to "up" at 0.40 conviction, tempered by flat 63d and negative 252d trend. Per the framework, hot realised inflation with anchored expectations resolves toward expectations, not a split — but here expectations are NOT anchored, they're also turning up. Last meeting I ran a modest widener on breakevens (+0.35, conviction 0.40), leaning on an "up" expectations call and a "down" inflation call. That is a direct hit on the premise of my prior position — the expectations driver, which the instructions say should dominate resolution when it diverges from realised inflation, has turned from supportive to neutral/soft-negative.

Realised inflation continues to show "down" (headline decelerating on base effects) but with core PCE stuck in a 5.1-5.6% choppy band — persistent but not accelerating. Labor tightness is flat, unemployment pinned at 3.60%, no wage-pressure signal to inform inflation persistence one way or the other, so no cross-read pushes expectations higher.

The evidence that justified the width has eroded — this is a genuine stale-view correction, not just a monthly wobble, since the analyst itself flagged its prior call as wrong. I'm cutting the position toward flat, tilting slightly negative to reflect the marginally negative medium-term momentum in breakevens, but keeping conviction low given the choppy, directionless recent prints.

arbitrated.notes: The relevant drivers for the breakeven wedge are inflation (realised) and inflation_expectations; balance_sheet, curve_slope, financial_conditions, and term_premium are real-yield/duration factors and out of scope for this instrument. Realised inflation continues to decelerate — headline down sharply to 6.40% y/y, MoM prints collapsing to 0.02%, though core PCE stays sticky at 5.21% and well above target on a 3m-annualized basis. This is the classic case flagged in the mandate: hot(ish)/sticky core vs. clearly cooling headline, but the read is 16 days old, not fully fresh. More importantly, inflation_expectations — the driver that actually prices into 10y breakevens — has flipped from my prior mild-down lean to a genuine up-lean: breakevens have grinded from 2.13 to 2.30, the last four prints rising steadily, and the series is now testing the top of its 2.20-2.40 consolidation range rather than the bottom. That is a real change in the expectations signal since last meeting, not a stale repeat. Per the panel-weighting rules, when realised inflation cools but expectations tick up, I resolve toward the expectations driver, not a split — and here expectations conviction is only moderate (0.35) and mid-range, not a breakout, so this doesn't justify a strong reversal, but it does undercut the case for continued narrowing that I held last time. Labor tightness is flat/stable, offering no independent inflation-persistence signal that would override the CPI cross-read. Given expectations turned up while the position is currently short breakevens, I need to close/soften the narrowing bet rather than add to it. I move from -0.10 toward flat-to-slightly-narrow, effectively near zero, reflecting the genuine divergence resolution but tempered conviction on the up-lean itself.

arbitrated.notes: Previous call was a small long-breakeven tilt (T10YIE +0.05, conv 0.25) built on realized inflation decelerating while expectations still leaned up. That expectations read has now flipped: breakevens round-tripped from 2.33 down to 2.24, hitting the exact falsifier level I set last time ("a drop back below 2.24... would restore the short-breakeven view"). The expectations analyst itself reversed from up(0.35) to down(0.45), citing two consecutive down-prints and negative 21d/63d momentum. Per the arbitration rule, expectations should dominate when realized inflation and expectations diverge, and here the divergence is now resolved in the same direction — both are pointed down, though realized inflation's own analyst flags a mild core-PCE persistence gap that keeps some upside risk to realized prints. But that persistence gap is a call on the level of inflation, not on whether breakevens (compensation) will widen given the actual market-priced measure is already softening. Labor market read is flat/tight but with no fresh signal — cycle-low unemployment, no acceleration, so it neither reinforces nor contradicts the breakeven direction; it's a background anchor at most, not a trigger for widening. Financial conditions easing (looser conditions) would typically be a mild breakeven-supportive force, but it's not the trade-relevant driver here — the direct, timely signal is the breakeven series itself via the expectations analyst, which is far more decision-relevant than the slower-moving balance-sheet/financial-conditions drivers already priced into the trend. Given the falsifier from last meeting has now triggered (sub-2.24 with a multi-observation decline), I flip the position from modest long-breakeven to modest short-breakeven, keeping conviction low-moderate since the level is still historically elevated and MoM CPI stayed hot enough to inject two-sided risk.

arbitrated.notes: The panel has moved against my prior narrow-breakeven view. Inflation expectations — the driver that actually matters for T10YIE — reversed: breakeven_10y made a fresh series high (2.43), with both 21d and 63d momentum clearly positive (+0.10-0.11pp), decisively breaching the 2.29-2.33 zone I had set as my own falsifier last meeting. That falsifier condition has been met explicitly, so honoring my own discipline requires flipping the expectations call from down to up. Realised inflation (headline CPI, core PCE) continues to decelerate, but this is the classic divergence case the framework flags: hot-vs-cool realised inflation should resolve toward the expectations read, not be split or averaged. Here it's actually the opposite alignment — inflation is cooling while expectations are firming — but the instruction is the same: expectations dominate the breakeven wedge. Labor tightness remains flat/range-bound with no loosening signal, giving no reason to expect wage-driven inflation persistence that would reinforce either side; I take no incremental view from it beyond noting it doesn't contradict the expectations turn. Balance-sheet, curve, financial-conditions, and term-premium reads are real-yield/duration signals and explicitly out of scope for the breakeven wedge — they're informative context (e.g., term premium creeping up) but not drivers of inflation compensation itself, so I exclude them from the trade decision. Given the clear break of my own stated flip condition and consistent short-term momentum in expectations, I am reversing the position from short breakevens to long breakevens, sized moderately given the longer 252d trend is still down and range position remains mid-low (0.34), which caps conviction.

Core PCE y/y has ground down only slowly (5.59→4.78) and remains 2.78pp above target, with core_minus_headline now negative — core is the sticky, persistent read and it is not falling fast. Given that a very favorable base-effect comparison is exhausted, and monthly momentum has stabilized rather than continued falling, I expect headline y/y to be roughly flat to slightly up at the next print rather than resuming its steep drop. This is a shift from my prior stance given the flattening in the yoy series and firming momentum data.
Would change its mind if: A headline MoM print below 0.15% or a fresh drop in core PCE y/y below 4.7% would confirm renewed disinflation and reverse this flat/up lean.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_3m_annualized, headline_mom_3m_avg, outgoing_mom, core_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The previous flat call held: the newest print (3.40%) sits at the 12-month low, not the loosening print that would have falsified it. In fact the series has moved tighter, not looser. Unemployment has drifted from 3.60-3.70% down to 3.40%, with unrate_change_3m/6m/12m all negative and the rate now at its 12-month low with zero range position (unrate_range_position=0.0) and 0.6pp below the ~4% natural rate estimate. Sahm-gap is a trivial +0.10pp, far from any recession-signal threshold, and job gains are described as robust and picking up, consistent with continued tightness rather than cooling. There is no evidence of loosening momentum anywhere in the data — the direction of travel over the past year has been toward a tighter labor market, not looser. Given the rate is already at the low end of its recent range and policy language emphasizes robust hiring, the most likely near-term outcome is unemployment holding near 3.40-3.50%, i.e., essentially flat, with perhaps a slight tilt toward staying low or ticking down marginally. I do not see support for a meaningful rise next month. I'll call this flat-to-slightly-tightening, leaning toward flat given one-month noise dominates at these small margins.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.60% would confirm loosening momentum; a print at or below 3.30% would suggest further tightening beyond recent range.

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y yield sits at 3.44, down from the prior print's trajectory and squarely mid-range (range_position 0.51). My prior call for "down" was directionally correct on net over the past month (21d change -0.11pp, 63d change -0.11pp), though the path was choppy — yield dipped to 3.40 then bounced to 3.53 before settling at 3.44. Neither of my stated flip triggers (rebound above 3.60, or break below 3.35) was hit, so the down lean stays intact but unconfirmed with force. The 10s30s slope at +0.23pp is modest and not signaling a sharp steepening that would indicate fresh term-premium building at the long end. Balance-sheet runoff continues per unchanged policy language, which is a modest structural upward pressure on long-end supply/term premium, but it hasn't been enough to push yields to new highs — the 252d change of +0.67pp shows the larger uptrend from a year ago, but the recent 21/63d windows show consolidation-to-mild-decline. Absent a decisive break either way, I lean toward continued mild softening but with reduced conviction given the choppiness and lack of a confirmed trend.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above 3.60 with positive 21d momentum and flattening 30s10s slope would signal renewed term-premium rise; a break below 3.35 confirms decline.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct and the trend has continued to weaken. The 10-year breakeven fell to 2.18 in the latest print from 2.22, extending a clear downtrend: -0.05pp over 21d, -0.30pp over 63d, -0.44pp over 252d. The series shows accelerating deterioration rather than stabilization — the last two readings (2.22, 2.18) are the lowest in the 10-observation window, and the range position of 0.12 (near the 12-month low of 2.10, far from the high of 2.76) confirms market-implied inflation expectations are compressing toward multi-month lows. Distance from the 2% target has narrowed to +0.18pp, the smallest gap in this window, meaning breakevens are converging toward — not diverging further above — the Committee's objective. There is no evidence of the rebound above 2.27-2.30 that would have falsified the downtrend; instead the trend accelerated further down. With no sign of stabilization in the short-window changes and the level sitting just 0.08pp above the 12-month low, momentum favors continued softening or at least no reversal. I maintain a down call, with slightly higher conviction given the accelerating pace and absence of any countervailing signal in the data shown.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above ~2.24-2.27 over the next few observations, breaking the accelerating downtrend, would revive a flat/up view.

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The last print (4.13% y/y headline, +0.16% m/m) came in below my prior falsifier threshold (0.15% m/m was the bar, and 0.16% is essentially at it), and importantly the y/y series shows a large drop from 4.95% to 4.13% — a 0.82pp deceleration in one month, driven by base effects as a high year-ago comparison rolled off. My previous flat call was too cautious; the disinflation continued more than I weighted. However, looking ahead one month, base effects matter enormously: the outgoing_mom being dropped from the y/y window was 1.26%, a very high comparison, which is why this print fell so much. The next comparison month is unlikely to be as favorable (headline_mom_3m_avg is 0.21%, not extreme), so the pace of y/y decline should moderate sharply from here — the easy base-effect gains are largely exhausted. Core PCE is at 4.79%, barely moved (core_yoy_change_3m only -0.15pp) and running 4.25% annualized over 3m, still well above 2% and above the 3m headline pace, indicating underlying persistence. Given headline momentum has stabilized (0.12-0.34-0.16% m/m range) rather than continuing to collapse, I expect next month's y/y print to be roughly flat to modestly higher, not a continuation of the sharp drop.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.45% would signal reacceleration; one below 0.05% would signal disinflation still has room, given fading base effects.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, core_3m_annualized, outgoing_mom, headline_mom_3m_avg

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The most recent print of 3.60% came in at the top of the trailing range, matching my prior "flat" call rather than breaking above it — so no loosening confirmation yet, but also no evidence of renewed tightening. Looking at the full series, the unemployment rate has oscillated tightly between 3.40% and 3.60% for 13 months with zero net drift over 3m, 6m, and 12m horizons. This is a textbook rangebound labor market: the 3m average (3.50%) sits right in the middle of the 12m range, and the Sahm-rule gap (0.10pp) is far below the 0.5pp threshold that would signal recessionary loosening. The level itself (3.60%) is comfortably below the ~4% natural rate proxy, indicating a still-tight labor market in absolute terms. Policy language continues to describe job gains as robust with unemployment "remained low," consistent with no material change in labor conditions. Given the lack of trend, the narrow historical band, and unemployment sitting at (not above) the recent high without follow-through, I see no basis for calling a directional shift. The next print is most likely to fall back within the established 3.40-3.60 band, consistent with noise rather than a turn.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.60% would confirm loosening momentum; a print at or below 3.30% would suggest further tightening beyond recent range.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_3m_avg

Over the past 10 observations the path rose from 3.57 to a peak of 3.83, then pulled back to 3.64 — a two-observation decline after the peak, not a sustained downtrend. My prior "down" call was wrong: the 21d change is +0.05pp (positive, not negative), and the yield remains well above the 3.35 level that would have confirmed decline. The 63d change of -0.44pp reflects the drop from a higher base earlier in that window, but the most recent momentum has stalled/reversed modestly. Range position at 0.63 shows the yield sits in the upper-middle of its 12-month range, not near the low. The 30s10s slope of +0.21pp is modestly positive but not sharply steepening, so no strong signal there. With ongoing balance-sheet runoff continuing to add supply pressure to the long end, and yield having only pulled back modestly from a fresh local high, I see this as more consolidation than a genuine reversal into decline. The evidence doesn't support a confident directional call; it's closer to flat-to-slightly-up given the failure to break lower and continued runoff.
Would change its mind if: A clean break below 3.55 with negative 21d momentum confirms decline; a push above 3.85 with steepening 30s10s confirms renewed rise.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes: The prior read was a -0.60 conviction bet on breakevens narrowing via anchored/falling expectations, with only mild support from realised inflation. This meeting's evidence has meaningfully softened that call. Inflation_expectations has moved from "down 0.60" to "flat 0.35": the 21d change is now +0.04pp (a stall/reversal from the slide), even though the 63d/252d trend and range position (0.23, near 12m low) still argue the broader downtrend hasn't reversed. This is a stabilization, not a re-acceleration — but it is a genuine change from last meeting's evidence, not noise, since it shows up in the analyst's own driver reassessment. Realised inflation also moved from flat/mild-up to flat: headline y/y fell sharply on base effects (now exhausted), while m/m and core remain sticky (~0.22-0.24% m/m, core 3m annualized above headline). This is the classic case where realised inflation is warm/sticky, but I weight the expectations read most since that's the wedge that actually prices breakevens — realised inflation only matters insofar as it feeds into forward expectations, and here expectations are NOT confirming reacceleration. Labor tightness stays flat/tight (3.60%, top of range) — informative as a cross-check that sticky core inflation has some wage-market support, reinforcing that expectations shouldn't collapse further, but it doesn't itself argue for widening breakevens. Given expectations have stopped falling but not turned up, and realised inflation is stabilizing rather than reaccelerating, the fair read is: the narrowing thesis has lost its edge. I should not carry the full -0.45 conviction from last meeting; the evidence for continued narrowing has weakened materially even though it hasn't flipped to widen. I resize toward a smaller, still-modestly-negative position reflecting reduced conviction rather than a reversal.

arbitrated.notes: Last meeting I held a modest narrow view (-0.20) on the premise that breakevens were still near the lower half of range with soft momentum. This meeting's evidence directly undercuts that. The inflation_expectations analyst reports breakevens jumped from ~2.22 to ~2.35 and have since held in a tight 2.35-2.39 band for six readings — 21d (+0.17) and 63d (+0.19) both solidly positive. That is precisely the falsifier condition I set last time ("a clear push above ~2.24-2.27 with 21d/63d momentum turning positive flips this to a widen view"). It happened and held rather than reverting, so my prior narrow thesis is falsified on its own stated terms. The analyst now calls flat-to-slightly-up because the last five prints have stalled in a 3bp band — momentum has faded but from an already-higher plateau, not reverted lower. Realised inflation ticked up (3.07%→3.29% yoy) on fading base effects with core still sticky, which is consistent with — not contradictory to — the expectations move; there's no divergence case here requiring me to override toward labor or realised inflation. Labor tightness is flat and unrevealing for breakevens directly, and per the panel's design realised inflation matters less than expectations when the two agree. Since the driver that actually moves breakevens (expectations) has re-based higher and stabilized there rather than round-tripped, my narrow position no longer has a basis. I should close the narrow bet and move to a small widen lean reflecting the plateau, not chase the full move since 21d momentum has clearly decelerated and this reads as consolidation, not a new impulse.

arbitrated.notes:
The core case for narrowing breakevens that I carried last meeting has weakened materially. Inflation expectations (breakeven_10y itself) is now reported flat with low conviction (0.25), explicitly reversing the prior "down" call — the analyst admits the last read's downtrend call was wrong, and the series has spent the whole window above 2.30, oscillating 2.33-2.39 with mildly positive 21d/63d momentum. That's close to my stated falsifier (rebound above ~2.30 with positive momentum) — not a full trigger, since the level was already there, but the momentum flip plus the explicit self-correction is a real signal I can't ignore. Realised inflation ticked down (3.72%→3.69%), essentially flat, with core PCE decelerating — the hot-inflation/anchored-expectations divergence that would justify holding a narrowing bet isn't present; instead both readings are subdued. Labor tightness is flat/plateauing, not tightening further, which removes the wage-persistence argument that supported my prior inflation call. There's no strong cross-read forcing an override of expectations here — if anything expectations and realised inflation are both drifting toward "stabilizing," which argues for flat breakevens, not continued narrowing.

Given expectations is the most trade-relevant driver for breakevens and it has moved from a confident "down" to a genuinely flat, low-conviction read, my prior -0.35 short-breakeven position should be trimmed sharply rather than held at conviction 0.50. I'll move to a small residual short, reflecting that realised inflation isn't accelerating and labor isn't re-tightening, but sized much smaller given the expectations anchor has stopped falling.

arbitrated.notes: This panel gives me relevant reads from inflation (realised) and inflation_expectations, with labor_tightness as a useful cross-read on persistence. Realised inflation is unambiguously disinflationary: headline yoy dropped sharply (3.69→3.25), core PCE continues a steady descent toward target-consistent pace, and the latest m/m print came in soft. That is a clear "down" signal on the realised side.
