This is the textbook divergence case the mandate calls out: hot-vs-cool realised inflation should resolve toward the expectations driver when they diverge, and here expectations are anchored while realised inflation is actually cooling — reinforcing rather than fighting the anchoring. Labor_tightness shows unemployment ticking up to 3.90% with clear momentum, a genuine loosening signal; softer labor markets support disinflation persistence and reduce upside risk to expectations, corroborating the anchored/falling inflation compensation view rather than contradicting it. None of this pushes me to override toward a hot re-acceleration story — there's no cross-read reframing persistence upward, only confirmation of cooling. Given anchored expectations and softening realised inflation plus loosening labor, the wedge should modestly narrow, not stay flat. I'll size a modest narrowing position, a bit larger than my prior since the disinflation and labor evidence has now firmed with fresh, in-range confirmations, while still respecting that expectations (the primary driver of breakevens) show no directional break — keeping conviction moderate rather than high.

arbitrated.notes:
The three drivers relevant to the breakeven wedge are inflation (realised), inflation_expectations, and labor_tightness (as a cross-read on persistence). Realised inflation continues to disinflate (headline yoy 3.13, core PCE 3.47, mom soft at 0.15%), though the analyst flags base effects turning less favorable going forward — a modest tempering, not a reversal. Inflation expectations (10y breakeven proxy) has stepped down to a new lower plateau (2.21-2.23) after an earlier drop, but has stabilized rather than continuing to fall — essentially flat at the new lower level, not trending further down. Labor tightness pulled back from its 3.90 spike to 3.70%, confirming the prior loosening signal was overstated; the market is only modestly loosening, well within a noisy band and Sahm gap still below trigger. This is a mild disinflationary cross-read for persistence, but not a strong one.

Per the resolution rule, when realised inflation drifts down while expectations are flat/anchored, I should resolve toward the expectations driver rather than split the difference or lean into the realised print. Expectations having plateaued (not falling further) is the key signal — the disinflation in realised prints is not translating into any incremental anchoring/de-anchoring at the expectations level. The labor cross-read reinforces "no urgency" rather than adding a fresh loosening impulse. None of this argues for a directional change in breakevens; if anything, the stabilization of expectations at a slightly lower plateau argues for a marginally smaller narrowing bias than previously held, since the down-move already happened and further downside is not confirmed.

I am reducing conviction and modestly resizing the narrow position, reflecting that the wedge-narrowing thesis has partly already played out (expectations already stepped down) and current data show stabilization rather than continued momentum in either direction.

discounted.why: Realised inflation drivers move breakevens less than expectations; disinflation here is already partly priced and losing momentum on base effects.

arbitrated.falsifier: A break of the expectations series below 2.18 (renewed downtrend) or back above 2.28 (re-anchoring higher) would flip this read; a hot CPI print reaccelerating yoy above 3.4% with rising expectations would also flip toward widening.

risks.text: Position is stale relative to a plateaued expectations series; further resizing risk if plateau proves temporary.
A dovish Fed pivot or renewed labor loosening could shift inflation expectations lower than current plateau suggests.
Breakeven carry is small; a modest narrow position may not compensate for whipsaw around the stabilized plateau.

trade.rationale: Expectations plateaued rather than falling further; realised disinflation losing momentum on base effects. Keep a smaller narrowing bet, reflecting reduced conviction versus prior meeting.

why.inflation: Headline yoy and core PCE continue easing, mom soft; base effects modestly less favorable ahead tempers strength of the call.
why.inflation_expectations: Series stepped down to lower plateau (2.21-2.23) but has stabilized, not still falling — mild residual downward lean only.
why.labor_tightness: Pullback from 3.90 to 3.70 confirms prior loosening was overstated; only mild residual loosening drift remains, low urgency.

Per the framework, expectations should dominate when the two diverge, and here they diverge clearly against the inflation print. The inflation analyst itself is unconvinced by its own hot print — core PCE continues decelerating (3.83→3.69→3.47→3.29), 3m annualized core is 2.31%, and the analyst frames the yoy uptick as base-effect noise with reduced conviction (0.30, flat call). That undercuts any case for treating the hot print as a persistence signal. The expectations read is fresh (formed this meeting) and reports the falsifier from my prior call was triggered (break below 2.18), but momentum has decelerated sharply (21d -0.05 vs 63d -0.23) and the series sits near its 12m low with no bounce yet — a lean-down, not a clear-down. Labor tightness continues to loosen (unemployment 3.70→3.80, drifting toward Sahm threshold), which is disinflationary at the margin and reinforces the anchored-to-lower expectations read rather than contradicting it — no gap to bridge here since inflation's own analyst already downgrades the hot print via core trends. Given expectations broke lower but deceleration in the pace of decline suggests limited further downside from here, I resize modestly toward continued narrowing but keep conviction contained — the big move already happened, and my position should reflect a persistence lean rather than a fresh acceleration call. I widen the negative position slightly from -0.08 to -0.12, reflecting the confirmed break lower in expectations, while not chasing the move given fading momentum.

arbitrated.notes:
The relevant drivers for the breakeven wedge are inflation_expectations and inflation, with labor_tightness as a cross-read on persistence. Inflation_expectations has stalled: after the earlier drift down, the last two prints are flat at 2.26, with 21d change actually positive (+0.10pp) — the decline that justified my prior -0.35 conviction call has clearly lost momentum and the analyst itself downgraded to flat/0.30. This is the single most important read for breakevens since expectations, not realised inflation, set the wedge. Realised inflation continues to soften (core PCE down to 3.11%, 3m annualized below target-consistent pace), reinforcing disinflation, but this is the classic case where hot-or-cool realised prints diverge from anchored/stalling expectations — the instruction is clear to resolve toward expectations, not split the difference. Labor tightness is genuinely flat/noisy (unemployment whipsawing 3.7-3.9%, Sahm gap well below trigger), giving no reinforcing signal either way for wage-driven inflation persistence — it neither confirms nor contradicts the expectations stall, so it's a weak cross-read this month, mostly reinforcing "no clear trend."

Given expectations have flattened rather than continuing to fall, my prior -0.35 conviction expectations call and the -0.12 breakeven short position are no longer well supported. The evidence for continued narrowing has weakened materially — this is exactly the "panel moved against the previous read" case. I'm cutting the short breakeven position roughly in half, reflecting reduced but not eliminated conviction that breakevens drift lower, since inflation is still soft and expectations show no upside momentum either, but the flattening kills the case for holding full size.

discounted.why: Noisy, non-trending; gives no reinforcing signal on inflation persistence this month.

Fed policy language shift toward higher-for-longer could re-anchor expectations either way depending on data.
Term premium and financial conditions are easing sharply, which could spill into breakevens via risk-on flows, not modeled here.

trade.rationale: Expectations have stalled while realised inflation still softens; case for narrowing weakened but not reversed, so I halve the prior short breakeven position.

The relevant panel for the breakeven wedge is inflation (realised), inflation_expectations, and labor_tightness as a cross-read on persistence. Inflation printed hot again (headline y/y 3.09→3.16, m/m accelerating to 0.41%, core PCE ticked up too), reinforcing last meeting's realised-inflation upturn — conviction 0.45 up, a genuine two-month momentum shift rather than a single print. But per the standing instruction, hot realised inflation with anchored expectations is the signature case to resolve toward expectations, not split. Expectations itself: 2.33, still chopping in the established 2.30-2.34 band, 252d change actually flat-to-down, range_position mid-pack. This is barely a "lean up" (0.30 conviction) and explicitly not a breakout — the analyst's own falsifier (break above 2.34) hasn't triggered. That's the key signal: the market is not re-pricing inflation persistence into expectations despite the hotter prints.

Labor_tightness offers a useful cross-read: unemployment continuing to drift up (3.90%, 12-month high) means labor market loosening, not tightening — this cuts against a wage-driven persistent-inflation story and supports the anchored-expectations read rather than validating the hot CPI as a regime shift. This closes the gap the inflation analyst couldn't address (wage pass-through) and reinforces staying skeptical of translating hot realised prints into breakeven widening.

Net: realised inflation heating up is real but not yet confirmed as unanchoring expectations, and softening labor tightness argues against sustained inflation persistence. I keep the modest narrowing bias from last meeting, essentially unchanged — the panel doesn't give me a "flip" trigger (no break above 2.34, no confirmed unanchoring), just marginal reinforcement of a range-bound expectations series with a hot-inflation offset. I slightly reduce conviction given the two-sided pull.

arbitrated.notes: Breakevens are the wedge between realised inflation and expectations, and this meeting's evidence is mixed but leans toward staying flat rather than widening further. Inflation itself (headline CPI m/m 0.22%, y/y ticking down 3.49→3.36) is decelerating at the margin even though 3m-annualized momentum remains elevated — the analyst calls this close to flat, tilting down. That's a softer read than my prior +0.55 conviction, which was based on hotter prints; the hot-print thesis has partially rolled over. More importantly, inflation expectations (the driver that should dominate the breakeven trade) printed flat at 2.41, after a stall following a longer uptrend — explicitly not breaking the 2.45 upside trigger nor the 2.38 downside trigger I set previously. This is a "stale-ish but not moved" read: conviction only 0.25, genuinely flat. Per the weighting rule, when realised inflation cools while expectations stay anchored, I should resolve toward the expectations driver — which itself says flat. Labor tightness plateaued at 3.90%, unchanged, a modest hawkish-tone data point but not moving the wage-inflation persistence story meaningfully; it's a mild cross-check supporting anchored expectations rather than a reason to shift breakevens either way. None of my prior flip triggers (breakeven sustained above 2.35/below 2.30, though the series is actually running near 2.41-2.43, above my stated band — worth noting the level context shifted but the *directional* signal is still flat) have been convincingly triggered — the series is just sideways at a higher plateau. Given expectations are flat, realised inflation is cooling (not accelerating), and labor is a non-mover here, the correct call is to stay flat on the breakeven wedge, maintaining no position.

arbitrated.notes: The relevant drivers for the breakeven wedge are inflation (realised) and inflation_expectations, with labor_tightness as a cross-read on persistence. Inflation_expectations reports a genuinely flat, low-volatility read: 10y breakeven range-bound 2.30-2.36, sitting mid-upper range, no momentum either way, and explicitly neither of my prior flip thresholds (2.45 up / 2.38 down reversal) was hit. This is a fresh, well-formed read directly on the instrument I trade, so it dominates. Realised inflation leans down (headline CPI y/y ticking lower, very soft 0.05% m/m, core PCE grinding toward target), but this is the classic case the framework warns about: hot-or-cool realised inflation without expectations moving is not a breakeven signal on its own — it's a real-yield/duration story, not a wedge story. Labor tightness is flat/plateaued at 3.90%, giving no independent push toward re-acceleration in inflation persistence that would unsettle expectations; it corroborates the anchored-expectations read rather than contradicting it. Balance-sheet taper (slower QT) is a slow, well-anticipated, largely priced driver — relevant to term premium/duration, not to the inflation wedge, so I exclude it here per the instruction to downweight slow financial-conditions inputs. Given expectations are explicitly flat and fresh, and the realised-inflation softness doesn't show up in expectations at all, the correct read is unchanged from last meeting: no clear directional edge in breakevens. I maintain the flat/no-position stance. Nothing in this panel constitutes the kind of persistence-vs-base-effects reframe that would justify overriding the expectations read with the realised-inflation read.

arbitrated.notes: Realised inflation continues to soften (headline CPI y/y 2.97%, negative m/m, core PCE decelerating) — this is the same disinflationary picture as last meeting, and it's a 15-day-old read, not stale but not fresh either. Last meeting I had expectations flat; this meeting's freshly-formed expectations read (conviction 0.40, "up") is the one driver that moved. Per the arbitration rule, hot/soft realised inflation diverging from expectations should resolve toward the expectations driver, and here expectations are the one showing an actual directional shift while realised inflation is just continuing its prior trend. Labor_tightness printed a genuine break higher (unemployment 4.10%, off a four-month plateau, Sahm-gap near recession threshold) — this is a labor-market loosening signal. Looser labor typically feeds through to expectations by removing wage-cost persistence, reinforcing disinflation risk, but it's a slower-moving cross-read and the panel's own expectations analyst already incorporates near-term momentum without citing labor as an input. So I use labor mainly as a corroborating (not driving) signal that realised inflation's downtrend is not about to reverse on wage pressure. Given expectations broke to a fresh high with real momentum, and realised inflation gives no reason to doubt that breakevens can grind higher, I flip from flat to a modest widening view on breakevens. Conviction is capped by the "choppy history" caveat the expectations analyst itself flagged and the fact we're not yet through the 12-month high (2.49).

Realised inflation (CPI/core PCE) is also confirmed lower, so both drivers now point the same direction — this is not the classic "hot inflation, anchored expectations" divergence case, it's coherent disinflation showing up in both actual prints and market-implied expectations. Labor tightness (rising unemployment, accelerating Sahm gap) reinforces the disinflationary backdrop by easing wage-driven persistence fears, supporting the expectations move rather than contradicting it. Given the level is now meaningfully above the recent low and off natural-rate anchor, and there's no sign of reversal back toward 4.00% or below, I lean toward continued softness but with reduced conviction versus a clean upward call, since the immediate momentum has stalled.

arbitrated.notes: The core wedge call rests on inflation_expectations vs realised inflation, with labor_tightness as a cross-read on persistence. Breakevens (10y BE) have stopped falling — the last several prints (2.07→2.15→2.16→2.15) show stabilization after a clear multi-horizon downtrend (21d/63d/252d all negative). The analyst shifted from "down" to "flat" with modest conviction (0.30), and explicitly flagged that a sustained move above 2.20 or below 2.10 would resolve the ambiguity. That's a genuine change from last meeting's "down" call, and per the framework a stale-to-freshening expectations read should be weighted heavily — it's the one that actually prices the wedge. Realised inflation is also "flat" now (was "down"): headline CPI's sharp drop looks partly base-effect driven and core PCE ticked up slightly, but this is described as mean-reversion after an outsized move, not a clean reacceleration signal. Labor tightness continues to loosen (unemployment at 4.20% for two months, top of 12m range) — a loosening labor market is disinflationary for wage-driven persistence, which argues against a hawkish inflation-expectations re-anchoring and supports keeping the wedge from re-widening. Combining a genuinely flattening (no longer falling) expectations series, a flattening-not-declining realised inflation picture, and a labor market that keeps loosening, the net signal is that breakevens have found a floor but lack a catalyst to widen. My previous -0.40 narrowing position was built on continued clear declines in both inflation and expectations; that thesis has weakened materially — expectations are now flat, not falling. I trim the narrowing conviction rather than flip, since nothing here supports outright widening.

discounted.why: Realised inflation now flat, largely base-effect noise; expectations dominate per framework when they diverge.

arbitrated.falsifier: A sustained break of BE_10y above 2.20 (widening) or below 2.10 (narrowing) over several weeks would confirm a new direction.

risks.text: Prior -0.40 narrowing bet may be stale; expectations series has already stopped confirming further narrowing.

Holding a shrunk short-BE position into a flat/ambiguous data regime carries costly whipsaw risk.

trade.rationale: Expectations have stopped falling and stabilized; realised inflation flat; labor still loosening. Trim prior narrowing bet sharply rather than flip, reflecting weakened but not reversed thesis.

Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  Headline CPI y/y fell to 2.43% from 2.61%, extending a multi-month decline and hitting a new 12m low, with headline_yoy_change_3m at -0.54pp. However the outgoing monthly print (0.21%) rose from the prior 0.16% and 0.05% readings, and headline_3m_annualized (2.17%) sits below the latest y/y print, meaning further y/y deceleration is not obviously locked in — the mom trend has actually firmed slightly (mom_gap_vs_outgoing +0.04pp). My prior "flat" call was wrong in direction terms: headline y/y did fall further (to 2.43%), continuing the down trend I had discounted. More importantly, core PCE y/y ticked UP for the second straight month (2.75 → 2.81 → 2.87), a genuine reversal in momentum after months of steady decline, and core_yoy_change_3m is now +0.10pp (accelerating), not decelerating. Policy language also shifted to "somewhat elevated" (softer than "elevated") and "some further progress" (downgraded from "modest further progress" — actually an upgrade in the disinflation narrative, but core PCE's turn up complicates that. Given headline's recent monthly firming (0.21% vs 0.05% two months prior) and core PCE's clear upward inflection, I lean toward headline CPI ticking up or flat next month rather than continuing its glide down.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print back below 0.05% or renewed core PCE y/y decline below 2.75% would restore the down/flat disinflation call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print ticked down to 4.10% from 4.20%, breaking a two-month plateau at the cycle high. That partially validates my stated falsifier condition (a drop back toward 4.00% would signal stabilization). The 3-month change is now flat (+0.00pp), even though 6m (+0.20pp) and 12m (+0.40pp) changes still show a loosening trend over the medium term. The Sahm-gap of 0.47pp remains below the classic 0.50 recession-signal threshold, and the level itself (4.10%) is only modestly above the natural-rate proxy of 4.0%. Policy language shifted to acknowledge "job gains have moderated, and the unemployment rate has moved up," which is a soft acknowledgment of the drift already visible in the data, not a signal of acceleration. My prior "up" call was directionally right on trend but this specific print reversed - a modest pullback, not a continuation of monotonic increases. Given the flattening 3m trend, the tick-down from the recent high, and range position still elevated at 0.80, I read this as a pause/stabilization rather than a resumption of tightening or a fresh loosening leg. I lean flat with a slight upward bias given the 6/12m trend context, but conviction is low given the single-month reversal.
Would change its mind if: A renewed rise back to 4.20%+ or two consecutive higher prints would confirm resumed loosening rather than stabilization.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_12m_high, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call (conviction 0.45) has not been vindicated by the near-term trend: dgs10 rose modestly to 3.81 from 3.75, but the 21d change is still slightly negative (-0.06pp) and the 63d/252d changes remain sharply negative (-0.67, -0.80pp), indicating the dominant multi-month trend is still one of falling long-end yields, not rising term premium. The yield sits near the bottom of its 12-month range (range_position 0.13, only 0.18pp above the 12m low of 3.63), which argues against a durable upward premium shift — this is a level consistent with continued anchoring rather than a breakout. The 10s30s slope is a modest 0.33pp, not indicating aggressive long-end cheapening. The last two prints (3.75→3.81) do show a short-term uptick, which keeps me from calling this outright down, but the broader momentum and range position dominate. Policy language is essentially unchanged substantively (same balance-sheet runoff continuation, just reworded), so no new signal there for term premium. On balance, the evidence is mixed: near-term uptick vs. persistent negative medium/long momentum and low range position. I lean toward a flat-to-slightly-up call with low-moderate conviction, acknowledging my prior thesis was too confident.
Would change its mind if: A clean break above 3.90 with positive 21d momentum and steepening 30s10s would confirm rising term premium; a drop back toward 3.70 would confirm down.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes: The relevant read for the breakeven wedge is the divergence between realised inflation and inflation expectations. The inflation analyst reports a genuine inflection: core PCE y/y has turned up for two straight months (2.75→2.81→2.87) and headline mom has firmed (0.21% vs 0.05%), even though headline y/y is still falling on base effects. That's a real signal of firming underlying inflation, not just a hot print. However, the inflation_expectations analyst — who is the primary driver of breakevens by construction — sees 10y breakeven pinned in a tight 2.12-2.19 band, near the middle of its 12-month range, with 63d/252d changes actually negative. This is the textbook "hot realised inflation, anchored expectations" case the framework flags: resolve toward the expectations reading, not a blend. The labor cross-read reinforces anchoring: unemployment ticked down to 4.10% from 4.20%, 3m change flat, Sahm gap still below the recession threshold — no acceleration in tightness that would feed a wage-price spiral to unmoor expectations. So the inflation uptick looks more like a base-effect/monthly-noise story than a structural persistence story, and market-implied expectations are not confirming it. I keep a mild bias toward inflation's own upside (it is a real inflection worth flagging) but for the breakeven trade itself I lean on expectations staying anchored to flat, with only a marginal upward tilt given the last two prints ticking to the top of the recent range. This does not justify resuming or expanding a narrowing bet; my prior -0.15 T10YIE position is now underweight-justified given anchored expectations, so I trim toward flat/small residual short.

discounted.why: Hot core PCE print alone, per framework, should not override an anchored expectations signal.

arbitrated.falsifier: A sustained break of breakeven_10y above 2.20 or below 2.10 over multiple weeks, or clear evidence core PCE persistence is feeding through to expectations.

risks.text: Fed easing bias could let realised inflation run hotter before expectations react, widening breakevens later.
Breakeven near top of recent short-term range; a small catalyst could trigger a real breakout either way.
Holding a short breakeven position against a firming core inflation trend carries negative carry risk if persistence proves real.

trade.rationale: Expectations remain anchored per the analyst closest to the instrument; trim prior -0.15 narrowing bet toward near-flat given lack of confirming breakout and offsetting inflation inflection.

why.inflation_expectations: Breakeven pinned 2.12-2.19, near range top but 63d/252d still negative; essentially anchored with slight upward tilt.
why.labor_tightness: Unemployment ticked down to 4.10%, 3m flat; no acceleration in tightness to unmoor expectations via wages.

arbitrated.notes: The relevant panel for the breakeven wedge is inflation, inflation_expectations, and labor_tightness; the balance_sheet, curve_slope, financial_conditions and term_premium reads are real-yield/duration factors and don't bear on the wedge itself, so I set them aside entirely for this instrument. Inflation (realised) reports a second straight month of reaccelerating headline mom (0.16→0.21→0.29) and a modest tick-up in core PCE y/y, confirming last meeting's "up" call and slightly firming conviction. But per the standing instruction, hot realised inflation against anchored expectations is the signature case to resolve toward expectations, not split. Inflation_expectations itself is nearly flat: the last two prints (2.34, 2.33) show the 21d/63d upward drift leveling off just under range highs, with the 252d trend still net negative. This is the freshest and most direct read on the actual wedge instrument (breakeven_10y), and it says stabilization, not a breakout. The labor cross-read (flat, with recent stabilization at 4.10% after a 3.70→4.20 climb) supports the expectations story: tightness isn't accelerating, so there's no fresh wage-cost pass-through to justify inflation persistence feeding into expectations. This overlap of anchored expectations and a stalled labor market argues against letting the hot CPI/PCE prints drive the breakeven call. My existing modest short breakeven bias (-0.05) reflected a similar read last meeting and the evidence hasn't meaningfully shifted — expectations are still flat-to-marginally-firmer, not breaking out. I'll hold the position essentially unchanged, perhaps trimming size slightly given expectations' modest upward tilt now outweighs its downward 252d trend, but not reversing.

discounted.why: Hot realised prints don't override anchored expectations per the panel's own resolution rule.

arbitrated.falsifier: A sustained break of breakeven_10y above 2.40 or below 2.20 over multiple weeks, or clear wage/labor-driven evidence that core persistence is feeding into expectations.

trade.rationale: Expectations remain flat-to-marginally-firmer despite hot realised inflation prints; labor stabilization removes persistence risk. Hold modest short breakeven bias unchanged from last meeting given no material shift in the wedge evidence.

arbitrated.notes: Realised inflation (headline CPI, core PCE) has firmed for three straight months with no deceleration signal — a clear "up" call at 0.60 conviction. But the expectations read, which is the one that actually prices the breakeven wedge, has flipped from my prior down (-0.45) to a modest up (+0.35): breakeven_10y bounced back above 2.30 (to 2.34), exactly the level I flagged previously as the falsifier for my narrowing view. That falsifier condition triggered. The expectations analyst itself describes this as choppy/range-bound rather than a breakout, and conviction is only moderate, but the signature "hot realised inflation, anchored expectations" divergence that justified my prior narrowing call is no longer present — expectations are now drifting up in the same direction as realised inflation, not staying anchored. Labor tightness is basically flat (unemployment oscillating 4.10-4.20, no fresh momentum), which removes the labor-market crosswind that previously supported a downside inflation-persistence override; it's neutral-to-mild-tightening pressure at most, not a strong counter-signal. Per the arbitration framework, when expectations and realised inflation both point the same direction, I resolve toward the expectations driver, and here that driver has moved from negative to modestly positive, undoing the basis for my -0.15 short breakeven position. Given the explicit falsifier condition (bounce above 2.30 for consecutive readings) has essentially been met, I'm no longer comfortable carrying the narrowing trade. I move to flat rather than flipping outright long, since expectations conviction is only moderate/choppy and I don't want to overreact to a single range-bound bounce.

discounted.why: Flat/oscillating print gives no directional lean strong enough to override the expectations signal.

arbitrated.falsifier: A drop back below 2.30 in breakeven_10y for two consecutive readings, or renewed clear anchoring of expectations, would revive the narrowing view.

trade.rationale: Prior narrowing thesis rested on expectations staying anchored while inflation ran hot; that anchor broke as breakevens rebounded above 2.30. With expectations now drifting up in the same direction as inflation, the wedge view is muddled — move to flat rather than carry a stale short or flip on a choppy signal.

arbitrated.notes: The two drivers relevant to the breakeven wedge are inflation (realised) and inflation_expectations. Realised inflation is confirmed hot and reaccelerating — headline CPI y/y to 2.99%, core PCE y/y 2.99%, with 3m annualized momentum running well above y/y, a fresh and well-supported read (0.62 conviction, momentum-led). Inflation expectations, however, remain the more trade-relevant driver for breakevens specifically, since breakevens are a market-priced expectations series, not a realised-inflation series. That read is itself confirmatory this round: breakeven_10y sits near its 12-month high (range position 0.93), momentum across 21d/63d/252d windows is consistently positive, and the analyst's own falsifier (sub-2.30 prints) never came close to triggering. So unlike the classic divergence case (hot realised inflation, anchored expectations → resolve toward anchored expectations), here both drivers point the same way: inflation running hot AND expectations drifting higher in tandem, near cycle highs, with no anchoring signal at all. That argues for widening, not narrowing, breakevens — a change from my prior flat stance, which was justified when expectations conviction was middling (0.35) and I wanted confirmation before taking a wedge view. Labor tightness gives essentially no signal (flat, low conviction) and doesn't inform the inflation-persistence read meaningfully here since unemployment is just oscillating in a narrow band near the natural rate — no cross-read override needed. Given expectations are actually the more direct read on the instrument I trade (breakevens), and they are now confirmed persistent and rising rather than merely a stale "maybe," I move from flat to a modest long breakeven position. Conviction is moderate — the expectations band is still tight (2.38-2.43) and not a clean breakout, so I keep sizing measured rather than maximal.

Core PCE y/y also dropped to 2.78 from 2.99, breaching my stated 2.85 flip level. So both of my falsifier conditions have essentially been met: headline momentum decelerated sharply and core PCE fell below 2.85. The 3-month annualized core figure (2.46%) is now much closer to target than the y/y level suggests, and headline 3m annualized (4.04%) is still elevated but driven by volatile components given core-minus-headline is near zero currently. Given the sharp single-month drop in both headitem and core, I was wrong last time to lean up. The 12m change of +0.30pp and sahm_gap of +0.20pp show mild cumulative loosening from the low, but the level remains only modestly above the ~4% natural rate estimate. Policy language shifted to describing the market as "stabilized at a low level" and "solid," which argues against reading this print as the start of a fresh deterioration trend — it looks more like normal month-to-month noise within a stable band. Given the range-bound history, I lean toward the next reading reverting toward the 4.0-4.1% area rather than extending the rise, but the recent jump raises some risk of continued upward drift.

arbitrated.notes: Both drivers I trade reversed from last meeting. Realized inflation flipped from "up 0.62" to "down 0.45" — headline CPI y/y fell to 2.80, core PCE y/y broke below my own 2.85 flip level to 2.78, and headline mom decelerated sharply. Inflation expectations flipped from "up 0.35" to "flat 0.20" — breakeven_10y actually fell from the 2.42-2.45 area to a low of 2.36 before a tiny two-print bounce to 2.37-2.38; 21d change is negative even though 63d/252d are still positive from the earlier run-up. This is the textbook divergence case flagged in my mandate, but not the signature one: here it's not "hot realized inflation, anchored expectations" — it's realized inflation cooling in the same direction expectations are stalling. Both drivers point the same way now: softer. Per my mandate, expectations is the trade-relevant driver for the breakeven wedge, and its own reading has gone from a modest up-lean to genuinely flat-to-slightly-down, with only a weak stabilization bounce that doesn't yet confirm a floor. Labor tightness stays flat/range-bound — the recent print bounced to 4.20% but is within a year-long 3.9-4.2% band with 3m/6m changes of 0.00pp, so it doesn't clearly reinforce or contradict the inflation persistence story either way; I don't lean on it here since neither inflation driver needs a labor cross-read to explain its move — both moved on their own hard data. Given expectations' own momentum is now negative-to-flat and realized inflation is confirming disinflation rather than fighting it, I no longer have grounds to hold a firm +0.40 wide-breakeven position. The evidence that justified that carry (both inflation and expectations leaning up) has flipped. I'm cutting the position toward flat/small positive, reflecting the stabilization bounce in breakevens but respecting that the trend that built my prior conviction has reversed.

arbitrated.falsifier: A push back above 2.42 on breakeven_10y with core PCE y/y rebounding above 2.95% would flip this back toward widening.

Flight-to-quality flows noted by term_premium analyst could compress breakevens for reasons unrelated to inflation expectations.
Fed holding rates steady removes a hawkish anchor; a dovish pivot could re-steepen inflation expectations quickly.
Cutting a carried long-breakeven position into a stabilization bounce risks being early if the pullback was noise.

trade.rationale: Both inflation and expectations reversed toward down/flat from last meeting's up leans; cut the +0.40 wide-breakeven carry to a small residual long given the tentative 2-print bounce, not a confident wide bet.

The 4-week change (-25,848) and 13-week change (-145,710) confirm QT is ongoing, with assets 699,305 below the 52-week high and no sign of reversal in the weekly series - every observation in the last 13 weeks is at or below the prior one. The key new information is the policy language: effective the following month, the Treasury redemption cap is being cut from $25B to $5B, a sharp slowing of the pace of decline, while the agency MBS/debt cap stays at $35B (though actual paydowns rarely hit that cap). This is not a pause or reversal - the Committee explicitly continues to reduce holdings - but the pace of runoff should decelerate materially going forward. So for month-end, I expect assets to still be lower than the current reading, since redemptions continue, but the magnitude of decline should shrink noticeably compared to the recent ~$25-30B/4wk pace, dominated by the now-larger relative role of agency/MBS runoff and slower Treasury paydowns. Given the single very low 0.03% mom print, some mean-reversion in the next headline y/y reading is likely (base effects won't repeat, and momentum had been running ~0.22-0.23% for months prior). Given the rate is unchanged month-over-month, the pace of increase has decelerated to near-zero over the past couple of readings, and the qualitative language reinforces stabilization, I read this as a flat labor market at a slightly elevated but not deteriorating level. arbitrated.notes: The relevant drivers for the breakeven wedge are inflation (realised) and inflation_expectations. Inflation: headline y/y fell sharply but the analyst flags a likely bounce off an unusually soft month, while core PCE ticked back above the 2.95% falsifier that would have flipped my prior read toward widening. That threshold was hit, but the analyst still frames the call as a headline-bounce story rather than a genuine reacceleration in underlying persistence, and the core series is oscillating in a range rather than trending. Labor tightness reinforces a benign read: unemployment is flat at 4.20%, drift has stalled, and policy language describes stabilization — no wage-pressure or tightening signal that would corroborate a durable inflation impulse. That's the cross-read the persistence question needs, and it comes back neutral, not hot. The real market mover for breakevens is inflation_expectations, and it has NOT flipped: breakeven_10y continues its slow grind higher (2.30→2.38), sitting near the top of its 12-month range, with none of the analyst's own stated triggers (below 2.35 sustained, or above 2.42) firing. Conviction there is still modest (0.30) but consistently up, not flat. My prior falsifier condition (breakeven above 2.42 AND core PCE above 2.95%) is only half-met — core PCE cleared its line but breakevens have not yet cleared 2.42. Given expectations dominate the wedge, realised inflation is ambiguous/mean-reverting, and labor gives no persistence confirmation, I lean toward maintaining a modest widening view, marginally reinforced by the expectations analyst's own upward lean this month. I'll keep the position essentially unchanged, perhaps nudging conviction up slightly given the corroborating expectations drift, but not sizing up meaningfully since neither trigger fully fired.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last three weekly prints show assets essentially flat-to-slightly-down (6,727,416 → 6,727,113 → 6,726,930), a sharp deceleration from the steady declines earlier in the series (e.g., 6,782,332 → 6,766,101 → 6,756,764). The 4-week change of -13,323M and 13-week change of -104,830M confirm runoff continued in the recent window, consistent with my prior "down" call being correct at the time. However, the policy language shows a material regime change: the Treasury redemption cap is being cut from $25B to $5B/month starting the coming period, an 80% reduction in the pace of Treasury runoff, while the agency MBS/debt cap stays at $35B (though actual MBS runoff rarely hits that cap in practice). This is a clear signal that the pace of decline will slow substantially going forward. Combined with the near-flat last two weekly readings, this suggests the balance sheet is transitioning toward a much slower drift lower rather than continued brisk runoff. I still expect a net decline over the next month given the cap is not zero and agency runoff continues, but the magnitude should be much smaller than the trailing 13-week pace implied.
Would change its mind if: A weekly rise in fed_assets reversing the trend, or confirmation that total runoff (Treasury+MBS) turns flat/positive after the new $5B cap takes effect.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was wrong: the last observation ticked to 0.57 from 0.54, and the series has been oscillating (0.59→0.55→0.55→0.56→0.54→0.57) in a modest but persistent uptrend since the low near 0.52. The 252d change of +0.83pp confirms this is a durable multi-month steepening trend from deep inversion toward flat-to-positive. The slowing of balance-sheet runoff (redemption cap cut to $5bn) is a technical support for front-end liquidity but doesn't materially alter this read. Given the spread sits near cycle highs with positive momentum, further steepening is more likely than reversal, though the last two prints (0.56→0.54→0.57) show some chop rather than a clean breakout, tempering conviction.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 0.50 with slope_change_21d turning negative would signal renewed flattening, reversing this steepening lean.

Call: up, conviction 0.40

Status: formed at this meeting.

The 4-week change (+0.04) confirms the tightening trend continued and did not reverse, consistent with my prior call. Conditions remain looser than average (distance from zero -0.38, still negative), but the trajectory is clearly one of steady tightening from very loose levels, now sitting near the top of its 52-week range (range_position 0.83). The last two prints being identical (-0.38, -0.38) suggests the pace of tightening may be decelerating or pausing, but there is no sign of reversal into renewed easing — no drop back toward -0.48/-0.51, and the 4-week change remains positive. Given the plateau in the last observation, I temper conviction slightly versus last time, but the multi-week momentum and range position still favor continued modest tightening (or at minimum a level shift already realized that persists) into month end rather than a reversal. Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:

The recent trajectory shows a peak at 2.30 followed by four consecutive declines/plateaus (2.30→2.27→2.25→2.23→2.23), consistent with the negative 21d/63d/252d change readings (-0.15, -0.17, -0.19 pp). That said, the last two prints are identical at 2.23, which could indicate stabilization rather than further decline. Breakeven remains 0.23pp above the 2% target, which is a modest anchoring point but not a strong pull in either direction absent fresh data. Given the clear multi-period downtrend but the apparent flattening in the very last two observations, I lean toward continued softness or flatness rather than a reversal back up. I do not see evidence supporting a renewed rise; the momentum, though decelerating, still points modestly lower or sideways into month-end.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above 2.27 sustained for multiple readings, or renewed acceleration in be_change_21d turning positive, would reverse this down/flat call.

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.35) was wrong: headline CPI y/y actually fell from 2.80 to 2.38 to 2.33, and the newest headline_mom print (0.16) sits above the 0.10% disinflation threshold I set but the y/y trend clearly turned down, not up. Headline is now at its 12-month low (2.33%, yoy_range_position 0.00), with headline_yoy_change_3m -0.67pp confirming a real deceleration in y/y terms, even though headline_3m_annualized (+1.69%) is below 2% target-consistent pace — actually quite soft. Core PCE is noisier: last three prints 2.78, 2.97, 2.67 — choppy, but the latest reading (2.67) is the lowest in the window, and core_yoy_change_3m is -0.32pp, also decelerating. However core_3m_annualized (+3.49%) is running hot on a 3-month basis, suggesting momentum could stabilize or firm the y/y core rate in the near term despite the recent dip. Headline_mom itself (0.16) is close to its 3-month average (0.14), not the low prints of two months ago, so the mom_gap_vs_outgoing (+0.09) hints at a firming base effect that could push next month's y/y flat-to-slightly up rather than falling further. Given the mixed short-term momentum (core 3m annualized hot) against still-declining y/y trend, I lean toward a flat-to-slightly-down call with modest conviction, correcting my prior overconfident "up" bias.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print materially above 0.30% or a rebound in core PCE y/y back above 2.9% would signal renewed acceleration, invalidating this flat/down call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_yoy_change_3m, headline_yoy_12m_low, yoy_range_position, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, core_3m_annualized, headline_mom, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The last three prints have been pinned at 4.20%, matching the 12-month high, after a dip to 4.00% two readings back. The 3-month change (+0.20pp) and 12-month change (+0.30pp) both show mild upward drift, and the range position sits at 1.0 (currently at the top of the trailing range), but the 6-month change is only +0.10pp, indicating the pace of loosening has not accelerated—this looks like a plateau at the cycle high rather than a fresh breakout. My prior flat call was essentially correct: the rate did not drop back toward 4.0% nor did it break clearly above 4.2%, it simply held there. Policy language still describes conditions as "stabilized at a low level" and "solid," and the shift to "risks roughly in balance" (from previous asymmetric framing) suggests the committee sees less urgency in labor slack, consistent with a plateau rather than acceleration. Given three consecutive flat readings at the high end of the range, with no confirmed break higher and no sign of reversal lower, the most defensible call remains flat, though the persistence at the 12-month high with sahm_gap of 0.30 keeps some modest upside risk in play.
Would change its mind if: A print above 4.2% (new 12-month high) confirms renewed loosening; a drop back toward 4.0% confirms stabilization/tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_6m, unrate_range_position, unrate_12m_high, sahm_gap, unrate_change_3m

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct: yields have continued to fall, from 4.32 to 4.17 over the last several observations, with 21d/63d/252d changes all negative (-0.06/-0.38/-0.53pp). The 10-year now sits at 4.17%, in the lower-middle of its 12-month range (range_position 0.47, well off the 4.79 high and only 0.54pp above the 3.63 low), consistent with easing term-premium pressure rather than a reversal. The 30s10s slope at 0.49pp is modest and not signaling aggressive long-end steepening. The main new policy input — slowing the runoff pace (cutting the redemption cap from $25bn to $5bn) — reduces the pace of Treasury supply hitting the market via balance-sheet runoff, which is a modest disinflationary-for-term-premium force (less net supply pressure), reinforcing rather than reversing the recent downward drift. Waller's dissent preferring the faster runoff pace is a minor hawkish counter-note but doesn't change the operative policy. With the trend intact, no break above 4.40, and reduced runoff-driven supply pressure, I lean toward continued softness or stabilization in the term premium, though the pace of decline has decelerated (21d change is small), suggesting the move may be flattening out near current levels.
Would change its mind if: A rise back above 4.35-4.40 combined with renewed 30s10s steepening would indicate the down-trend has reversed into fresh term-premium pressure.
Leaned on: dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s, dgs10_level

arbitrated.notes: The relevant instrument here is 10y breakeven itself, and the expectations analyst — who tracks that series directly — now reports a clear reversal from the prior meeting: breakeven_10y has drifted from 2.30 down to 2.23, with negative 21d/63d/252d momentum, though the last two prints are flat at 2.23, suggesting the decline is decelerating rather than reversing. This directly contradicts my prior carried position (+0.12 T10YIE, built on an "up" expectations call that the analyst itself now says was wrong). Realised inflation confirms the same direction: headline CPI yoy has fallen to a 12-month low (2.33%) with clear deceleration, and core PCE's latest print (2.67%) is also the softest in the window, despite a hot 3m-annualized core figure. This is not the classic divergence case (hot realised inflation, anchored expectations) — both drivers here are pointing the same way, softer, which reinforces rather than complicates the read. Labor tightness is flat/plateaued at cycle-high unemployment (4.20%), which is consistent with easing wage pressure and gives no reason to expect inflation persistence resisting the breakeven's downdrift; if anything it's a mild disinflationary cross-read supporting the expectations call. Since expectations is the direct, freshest, most trade-relevant read (formed at this meeting, on the actual instrument I trade) and it has flipped from up to down, I should flip my position too. The flattening in the last two prints tempers conviction — this isn't a strong breakdown, just a stabilization at a lower level — so I move to a modest narrowing position rather than a large one, closing out the prior widening stance.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct: total assets fell again over the last 4 weeks (-36,033 $M) and 13 weeks (-92,857 $M), continuing a steady runoff trend that has taken the balance sheet 585,730 $M below its 52-week high. However, the policy language shows a material deceleration ahead: effective [date] the Treasury redemption cap drops from $25B/month to $5B/month, while the agency MBS/debt cap stays at $35B. This is a substantial slowing of the pace of QT (potential total cap runoff falls from up to $60B/month to as low as $40B/month, though actual paydowns are often below caps). The direction remains runoff (down) — assets should still decline into month-end — but the recent 4-week and 13-week pace (~$9-30B/month) already looks close to or even below the new combined cap ceiling, so continued decline is likely but at a decidedly slower rate than the trailing 52-week pace (-611,075 $M) would suggest. I maintain a "down" call for next month-end, but with reduced conviction given the explicit policy shift toward a much slower runoff pace, and note Waller's dissent shows the taper itself was contested, underscoring this is a genuine regime change, not just noise.
Would change its mind if: A weekly rise in fed_assets, or evidence that actual Treasury+MBS runoff has flattened to near-zero under the new $5B/$35B caps.

  Given the plateau near range highs with only a marginal recent pullback, I lean toward continued mild steepening but with reduced conviction versus last time, since the near-term momentum has clearly stalled compared to the strong 63d/252d trends.

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up/tightening, 0.40) was wrong: the series continued easing after the interim peak. From the recent trough of -0.38 the index has fallen for four straight readings (-0.38, -0.39, -0.40, -0.42, -0.44, -0.45), a clear renewed easing trend, exactly the reversal condition I flagged as my falsifier. The 4-week change is now -0.06 (easing), while the 13-week change is only +0.04, reflecting the mid-window bounce that has since fully reversed. Conditions remain well below zero (-0.45), i.e., looser than historical average, and are now closer to the 52-week low (-0.51) than the high (-0.36), with range position at 0.36 and falling. Momentum is the dominant signal here: three to four consecutive weekly declines outweigh the stale 13-week average. With no policy language contradicting this and no offsetting evidence, I read this as a resumption of the easing trend that had only paused mid-series. I am revising toward down (easing further) for month-end, with moderate conviction given the streak is short and could stall again as it did before.

Levels remain elevated relative to the 12-month range: be_range_position at 0.73 shows breakeven sits in the upper third of its year-long band, and distance from the 2% target is a still-meaningful +0.34pp. The last two readings (2.32, 2.32, 2.34) show a modest but real uptick after several months of gentle softening. Given the acceleration in the 21-day change and the proximity to the 12-month high (2.46) rather than the low (2.02), the more consistent read now is that inflation expectations are stabilizing or drifting mildly higher, not continuing to fall. I do not have strong conviction this is a sustained re-acceleration, but the flat-to-down thesis from last month is no longer well supported by the most recent print.

Given the level is now 0.3pp above the 4% natural-rate anchor and the trend has broken out of its prior band, I lean toward continued modest loosening over the next month, though the magnitude is still modest.

arbitrated.notes:
Last meeting I called breakevens narrowing on both realised inflation (mild) and expectations (the dominant driver, -0.35). This meeting's expectations read directly reverses: breakeven_10y ticked back up (2.32→2.34), be_change_21d flipped to +0.11pp from negative, and the level sits at 0.73 of its 12-month range, near highs rather than lows. That is a fresh, live print on the exact instrument I trade, not a stale or noisy signal — it's the analyst whose job is to read T10YIE itself. Per the arbitration rules, expectations dominate when realised and expectations diverge, and here they still diverge but the divergence direction has flipped versus last time: inflation (realised) is now flat-to-cooling (headline decelerating, core PCE drifting down to 2.61%, 3m annualized just 1.18%), while expectations are drifting up. This is the mirror image of the classic "hot realised, anchored expectations" case — here realised is cooling but expectations are firming — and the rule is clear that expectations should be weighted most heavily on divergence, so I resolve toward expectations widening, not toward the cooling inflation print.

Labor tightness ticked up to a new 12-month high (4.30%), consistent with loosening labor markets, which is inflation-cooling at the margin and reinforces the realised-inflation softness — but this is a slow-moving, well-anticipated driver for the breakeven wedge and doesn't override a live breakeven print.

Given this, my prior -0.35 short-breakeven position was built on a since-reversed expectations signal. The evidence has moved against me, and the panel gives a concrete, on-instrument reason to flip: I should reverse the position from narrowing to modestly widening, sized down given the analyst's own conviction is moderate (0.35) and describes this as tentative stabilization/uptick, not a strong re-acceleration.

discounted.why: Realised inflation cooling but subordinate to the live, on-instrument expectations reversal per divergence rule.
Slow-moving cross-read supporting realised-inflation softness; not decisive for the expectations-driven wedge call.

arbitrated.falsifier: A drop back below 2.32 sustained for multiple readings, or be_change_21d turning negative again, would revert this to a narrowing call.

risks.text: Reversing a carried short-breakeven position on a single moderate-conviction uptick risks whipsaw if the print reverts.
Fed balance-sheet taper slowdown and dovish risk-of-unemployment language could independently lift inflation expectations, confounding the read.
Breakevens have oscillated in a range for months; this could be another false stabilization signal like the one that flipped inflation_expectations' own prior call.

trade.rationale: Expectations analyst's live breakeven reversal (higher 21d change, upper-range level) outweighs cooling realised inflation; flip prior -0.25 short to modest long, sized to moderate conviction.

why.inflation_expectations: Breakeven_10y reversed higher (2.32→2.34), 21d change flipped to +0.11pp; direct, live read on the traded instrument.
why.inflation: Headline decelerating, core PCE cooling to 2.61%, 3m annualized 1.18%; realised inflation drifting lower.
why.labor_tightness: Unemployment at new 12m high (4.30%), loosening labor market; reinforces cooling realised-inflation backdrop, not decisive for wedge.

arbitrated.notes: The relevant drivers for the breakeven wedge are inflation (realized) and inflation_expectations, with labor_tightness as a cross-read on persistence. This month's pattern is the textbook divergence case: headline CPI keeps firming (2.68%→2.74% yoy) while the breakeven series itself has rolled over for five straight readings (2.45→2.39), even though the 21d change is still technically positive. Per the arbitration rule, when realized inflation runs hot but expectations stay anchored (or here, actively rolling over), I resolve toward the expectations driver, not a split. The inflation analyst's own report undercuts the hawkish read anyway — the 3m-annualized pace (2.35%) is running below the yoy print, meaning the recent yoy uptick is base-effects mechanics rather than fresh momentum, which is exactly the kind of override condition (persistence vs base effects) that should shift weight toward expectations. Labor tightness ticking up to a 12-month high in unemployment (loosening, not tightening) is a modest disinflationary cross-read that reinforces the expectations pullback rather than contradicting it — it does not support a hot-persistence story that would flip me back toward inflation. Expectations conviction is only moderate (0.35) because the medium-term drift (63d, 252d) is still positive and one hot print could reverse the recent rollover, but the near-term trajectory is the more decision-relevant signal for a month-ahead breakeven call. I'm flipping from my prior mild widen (+0.05) to a mild narrow, sized modestly given the moderate conviction on both sides and the explicit conflict between short and long-window expectations momentum.

discounted.why: Own data shows base-effect distortion; realized hot print not confirmed by annualized pace, so expectations dominate.

The wedge trade rests almost entirely on inflation_expectations, and this meeting's read is a genuine change: breakevens have stabilized at 2.41 after a modest pullback from 2.43, with 21d change now flat (+0.02) rather than negative. Here expectations are not rolling over, but they're also not accelerating — they're flat-to-firm at an elevated plateau. That's a meaningfully different signal from broad narrowing.

Given: (1) my expectations-driven narrowing thesis was falsified, (2) the breakeven series itself is flat/stable rather than falling, and (3) hot realised inflation plus firming labor tightness put a floor under further narrowing, I no longer have basis for a -0.20 narrowing position. arbitrated.notes:
The relevant panel for the breakeven wedge is inflation, inflation_expectations, and labor_tightness. Realised inflation (headline CPI) is flat/plateauing at cycle highs (2.99%) while core PCE is decelerating (2.75%, falling 3m annualized to 2.60%) — a softening-persistence signal, not a hot-print story. Labor tightness continues to loosen (unemployment up to 4.50%, 12m high, accelerating pace over 3m), which via the Phillips-curve channel argues for less wage-driven inflation persistence ahead, reinforcing the disinflationary read on realised inflation rather than offsetting it.

The decisive driver is inflation_expectations, which has moved materially since last meeting: 10y breakevens have broken below my own stated falsifier (sustained sub-2.25 for six straight readings, now 2.22-2.23), with negative 21d/63d/252d momentum and range position near 12-month lows. This is no longer a stale or ambiguous read — it's a live break in the direction my prior position was already leaning, and it now has directional confirmation over multiple readings. The panel's signature case (hot realised inflation, anchored expectations) does NOT apply here — instead we have realized inflation plateauing/decelerating (core) AND expectations grinding lower in tandem, which is a coherent, reinforcing narrative for narrower breakevens rather than a divergence requiring an override.

Labor loosening cross-reads into inflation persistence: a loosening labor market reduces wage-driven inflation risk, supporting the case that realised inflation's plateau is more likely to roll over than reaccelerate, which itself supports anchoring/further softening of expectations rather than a re-acceleration scare.

Given the expectations break exceeded my prior flip threshold and momentum, though decelerating, remains net negative, I resize the position modestly larger short breakevens rather than merely holding, but stay cautious since the last three prints show deceleration in the decline (2.22, 2.23, 2.23) — a shallow drift, not a violent break.

arbitrated.notes:
The prior call (breakevens narrowing, -0.25 T10YIE) was built on inflation_expectations at -0.35 with labor_tightness at +0.55 informing persistence. This meeting's evidence undercuts that setup materially. My own stated falsifier — a print at/above 2.25 — has now fired: breakevens printed 2.25, the top of the 10-point window. The expectations analyst itself has downgraded from "down" to "flat" (conviction only 0.30), explicitly noting the medium-term drift (63d/252d both -0.10pp) is being offset by a fresh short-term uptick, and calling this "stabilization rather than resumption of decline." That is a genuine turn in the driver that matters most for this trade, not noise to be argued away.

Realised inflation is unchanged at flat (headline plateaued at 3.00% y/y, core decelerating), consistent with last meeting — no acceleration surprise to force a re-think there, and per the playbook realised inflation is subordinate to expectations when they diverge.

Labor tightness ticked down in its own print (4.40% from 4.50%) but the analyst still calls "up" with reduced conviction (0.30, down from 0.55 implied last time), citing Sahm gap and 12m trend. This is a softer read than before — less support for a persistence/reacceleration story that would keep breakevens compressed. The cross-read from labor into inflation persistence is now weaker, not stronger, further undermining the case for continued breakeven narrowing.

Given expectations flipped from down to flat (its own falsifier triggered), and labor conviction on "up" weakened, the informational basis for holding a meaningful narrowing bet has eroded. I'm resizing the short breakeven position toward near-flat/small residual short, reflecting stabilization rather than a resumption of the decline, while not yet flipping long given genuine flat/mixed evidence rather than a confirmed reversal.

discounted.why: Realised inflation flat and subordinate to expectations per divergence rule; no new signal.

arbitrated.falsifier: A confirmed sustained push above 2.26 over two readings would flip to a long breakeven view; a drop back below 2.22-2.23 would re-confirm narrowing.

risks.text: Fed ending QT and starting short-term purchases could shift inflation expectations anchoring in ways not yet captured.
Breakeven print at range high may reflect technical/liquidity noise rather than genuine expectations shift.
Holding a shrunk short breakeven position still bleeds if stabilization proves temporary and expectations resume declining.

trade.rationale: Expectations driver flipped from down to flat, triggering its own falsifier; residual small short reflects stabilization, not confirmed reversal, so position is trimmed sharply from -0.25.

why.inflation_expectations: Analyst flipped down→flat; own falsifier (print≥2.25) triggered; no clear directional edge now.

why.labor_tightness: Print fell to 4.40% but analyst still leans up with much lower conviction; weaker persistence cross-read than before.
